[8] I know what you've done. Look — I've set an open door in front of you that no one can close. You have only a little strength, but you've kept my word and you have not denied my name.

[9] Watch what I do with the synagogue of Satan — those who claim to be Jews but are lying. I will make them come and bow at your feet and finally see that I have loved you.

[10] Because you kept my command to endure, I will keep you through the hour of testing that's coming on the whole world to expose everyone who lives on the earth.

[11] I'm coming soon. Hold tight to what you have. Don't let anyone strip you of your crown.

[12] The one who conquers — I'll make a pillar in the temple of my God. They'll never leave it. I'll write on them the name of my God, and the name of my God's city, the new Jerusalem coming down out of heaven from my God, and my own new name.

[13] If you have ears, hear what the Spirit is saying to the assemblies.

[14] To the messenger of the assembly in Laodicea, write:

These are the words of the Amen, the faithful and true witness, the origin of God's creation:

[15] I know what you've done. You're neither cold nor hot. I wish you were one or the other.

[16] But because you're lukewarm — neither hot nor cold — I am about to spit you out of my mouth.

[17] You keep saying, "I'm rich, I've made it, I need nothing." You have no idea that you are the wretched one, the pitiful one, poor and blind and naked.

[18] So here's my counsel: buy from me gold refined in fire so you can actually be rich, and white clothes to cover the shame of your nakedness, and salve for your eyes so you can finally see.

[19] The ones I love — I confront them. I discipline them. So burn with seriousness. Repent.

[20] Look — I'm standing at the door. I'm knocking. If anyone hears my voice and opens the door, I will come in, and we will eat together — them with me, me with them.

[21] The one who conquers — I'll give them a seat with me on my throne, just as I conquered and sat down with my Father on his throne.

[22] If you have ears, hear what the Spirit is saying to the assemblies.

[1] After this I looked, and there it was—a door standing open in heaven. And the voice I had heard before, the one like a trumpet blast, called to me: "Come up here. I'll show you what has to happen next."

[2] Instantly I was in the Spirit. And there before me stood a throne in heaven, with Someone seated on it.

[3] The One on the throne blazed like jasper and carnelian, and a rainbow circled the throne, glowing like emerald.

[4] Around the throne stood twenty-four other thrones, and on them sat twenty-four elders robed in white, crowns of gold on their heads.

[5] From the throne came lightning, rumbling, thunder. Seven torches of fire burned in front of it—the seven Spirits of God.

[6] And before the throne stretched something like a sea of glass, clear as crystal. In the center, around the throne, were four living creatures, covered with eyes—front and back.

[7] The first like a lion. The second like an ox. The third with the face of a man. The fourth like an eagle in flight.

[8] Each of the four had six wings, and they were studded with eyes, inside and out. Day and night they never stop saying:

"Holy, holy, holy is the Lord God Almighty,
who was, and is, and is coming."

[9] And whenever the living creatures give glory and honor and thanks to the One seated on the throne—the One who lives forever and ever—

[10] the twenty-four elders fall down before Him. They worship the One who lives forever. They cast their crowns before the throne and cry out:

[11] "You are worthy, our Lord and God,
to receive glory and honor and power.
For You created all things—
by Your will they exist,
by Your will they were made."

[1] Then I saw it—in the right hand of the One on the throne—a scroll, written front and back, sealed shut with seven seals.

[2] And a mighty angel was calling out, voice ringing across everything: "Who is worthy to break the seals and open the scroll?"

[3] Silence. No one in heaven, no one on earth, no one beneath the earth could open it. No one could even look inside.

[4] I wept. I wept hard, because no one was found worthy to open the scroll or to see what it held.

[5] Then one of the elders said to me, "Stop weeping. Look—the Lion from Judah's tribe, the Root of David, has triumphed. He can open the scroll and break its seven seals."

[6] So I looked. And there, at the center of the throne, surrounded by the four living creatures and the elders—a Lamb. Standing. Slaughtered. With seven horns and seven eyes, which are the seven Spirits of God sent into all the earth.

[7] He stepped forward and took the scroll from the right hand of the One on the throne.

[8] The moment He took it, the four living creatures and the twenty-four elders fell facedown before the Lamb. Each one held a harp and a golden bowl full of incense—the prayers of the holy ones, rising.

[9] And they sang a new song:

"You are worthy to take the scroll
 and break its seals,
because You were slaughtered,
and with Your blood You bought for God
people from every tribe and tongue,
every nation and race—

[10] You made them a kingdom and priests for our God,
and they will reign over the earth."

[11] Then I looked, and I heard the voices of countless angels surrounding the throne and the living creatures and the elders—thousands upon thousands, ten thousand times ten thousand—

[12] thundering together:

"Worthy is the Lamb who was slaughtered—
worthy to receive power and wealth
and wisdom and strength,
honor and glory and blessing!"

[13] And then every creature—in heaven, on earth, under the earth, in the sea, everything that breathes—I heard them all, together:

"To the One on the throne and to the Lamb:
blessing and honor and glory and power
forever and ever!"

[14] And the four living creatures said, "Amen." And the elders fell down and worshiped.

[1] I watched as the Lamb broke the first of the seven seals, and I heard one of the four living creatures speak with a voice like thunder: "Come."

[2] I looked, and there — a white horse. Its rider held a bow, was handed a crown, and rode out conquering, bent on conquest.

[3] He broke the second seal, and I heard the second living creature say: "Come."

[4] Out came another horse, this one fiery red. Its rider was given authority to strip peace from the earth, to make people slaughter each other. A great sword was placed in his hand.

[5] He broke the third seal, and I heard the third living creature say: "Come." I looked, and there — a black horse. Its rider held a pair of scales.

[6] And from somewhere among the four living creatures I heard a voice: "A day's wages for a loaf of bread. A day's wages for three loaves of barley. But the oil and the wine — leave them alone."

[7] He broke the fourth seal, and I heard the voice of the fourth living creature say: "Come."

[8] I looked, and there — a sickly pale horse. Its rider was named Death, and Hades trailed close behind him. They were given authority over a quarter of the earth — to kill by sword, by famine, by plague, by the wild beasts of the land.

[9] He broke the fifth seal, and I saw beneath the altar the souls of those who had been slaughtered for the word of God and for the testimony they had held to.

[10] They cried out with a great voice: "How long, Sovereign Lord — holy, true — until you judge? Until you avenge our blood on the people of the earth?"

[11] Each was given a white robe, and they were told to rest a little longer, until the full number of their fellow servants and brothers and sisters — those marked for death just as they had been — was complete.

[12] Then I watched him break the sixth seal. A massive earthquake shook the earth. The sun went black like sackcloth made of hair. The whole moon turned the color of blood.

[13] The stars of heaven dropped to the earth like a fig tree shaking off unripe figs in a hard wind.

[14] The sky peeled back like a scroll rolling up. Every mountain and island was wrenched from its place.

[15] Then the kings of the earth, the warlords, the generals, the rich, the powerful — every slave and every free person — fled into caves and crammed themselves into the cracks of mountains.

[16] They screamed at the mountains and the rocks: "Fall on us! Hide us from the face of the One on the throne, and from the wrath of the Lamb!

[17] The great day of their wrath has come — and who can stand?"

[1] After this I saw four angels stationed at the four corners of the earth, gripping the four winds, holding them back so no wind could touch the land, the sea, or any tree.

[2] Then I saw another angel rising from where the sun comes up, carrying the seal of the living God. He called out — a voice that cut — to the four angels who'd been given power to ravage land and sea:

[3] "Don't touch the land. Don't touch the sea. Don't touch a single tree. Not until we've marked the servants of our God on their foreheads."

[4] And I heard the number of the sealed: one hundred and forty-four thousand, drawn from every tribe of Israel.

[5] From Judah, twelve thousand sealed. From Reuben, twelve thousand. From Gad, twelve thousand.

[6] From Asher, twelve thousand. From Naphtali, twelve thousand. From Manasseh, twelve thousand.

[7] From Simeon, twelve thousand. From Levi, twelve thousand. From Issachar, twelve thousand.

[8] From Zebulun, twelve thousand. From Joseph, twelve thousand. From Benjamin, twelve thousand sealed.

[9] Then I looked — and there it was: a crowd nobody could count. Every nation. Every tribe. Every people. Every language. Standing before the throne and before the Lamb, draped in white, palm branches in their hands.

[10] And they were shouting, the sound rolling like thunder: "Salvation belongs to our God on the throne — and to the Lamb!"

[11] All the angels were ringed around the throne, around the elders, around the four living creatures. They dropped to their faces before the throne and worshiped God,

[12] saying: "Yes. Amen. Blessing and glory and wisdom, thanksgiving and honor, power and strength — to our God forever and ever. Amen."

[13] One of the elders turned to me and asked, "These people in white — who are they? Where did they come from?"

[14] I said, "Sir — you would know." And he told me: "These are the ones who came through the great tribulation. They washed their robes. They made them white in the blood of the Lamb.

[15] That's why they stand before the throne of God and serve him day and night in his temple. And the One on the throne will spread his shelter over them.

[16] They will never be hungry again. Never thirsty again. The sun will not beat on them. No scorching heat.

[17] Because the Lamb at the center of the throne will shepherd them. He'll lead them to springs of living water. And God himself will wipe every tear from their eyes."

[1] When the Lamb broke the seventh seal, heaven fell silent. For about half an hour — nothing.

[2] Then I saw the seven angels who stand before God, and seven trumpets were placed in their hands.

[3] Another angel came and stood at the altar, holding a golden censer. He was given vast quantities of incense to offer, mingled with the prayers of all God's holy ones, on the golden altar before the throne.

[4] The smoke of the incense, carrying those prayers, rose from the angel's hand straight into the presence of God.

[5] Then the angel took the censer, filled it with fire from the altar, and hurled it down to the earth. Thunder erupted. Voices. Lightning. The ground shook.

[6] And the seven angels with the seven trumpets lifted them to their lips, ready.

[7] The first sounded. Hail and fire mixed with blood crashed down on the earth. A third of the earth burned. A third of the trees burned. Every blade of green grass — burned.

[8] The second angel sounded. Something like a massive mountain, blazing with fire, was hurled into the sea. A third of the sea turned to blood.

[9] A third of every living thing in the sea died. A third of the ships — destroyed.

[10] The third angel sounded. A great star fell from the sky, burning like a torch, and it dropped onto a third of the rivers and onto the springs of water.

[11] The star's name is Wormwood. A third of the waters turned bitter, and many people died from drinking what had become poison.

[12] The fourth angel sounded. A third of the sun was struck, a third of the moon, a third of the stars — all of them darkened. A third of the day lost its light, and the night the same.

[13] Then I looked, and I heard an eagle flying overhead through the middle of the sky, crying out: "Woe — woe — woe to those who live on the earth, because of the trumpet blasts still to come from the three remaining angels."

[1] The fifth angel sounded. I saw a star that had crashed from heaven to earth, and he was handed the key to the shaft of the abyss.

[2] He opened it. Smoke poured up out of the pit like the exhaust of some massive furnace — sun blackened out, sky choked dark by the fumes rising from below.

[3] Out of that smoke locusts swarmed onto the earth, armed with power like the sting of scorpions.

[4] They were ordered: don't touch the grass, don't touch any green thing, don't touch a single tree. Only the people who lack God's seal on their foreheads.

[5] They weren't allowed to kill — only to torture, and only for five months. The pain they inflicted was the searing pain of a scorpion's strike when it nails human flesh.

[6] In those days people will hunt for death and won't find it. They'll beg to die, and death will run from them.

[7] The locusts looked like warhorses ready for battle. On their heads, what looked like crowns of gold. Their faces — human faces.

[8] Hair like a woman's. Teeth like a lion's.

[9] Iron breastplates across their chests. The roar of their wings was like the thunder of chariots and horses charging into war.

[10] Tails like scorpions — stingers and all — and in those tails their authority to torment humanity for five months.

[11] They have a king over them: the angel of the abyss. His name in Hebrew is Abaddon. In Greek, Apollyon. Destroyer.

[12] One catastrophe down. Two more inbound.

[13] The sixth angel sounded. A voice came out from the horns of the golden altar before God,

[14] telling the sixth angel with the trumpet: "Release the four angels chained at the great river Euphrates."

[15] So they were unleashed — the four angels held in reserve for this precise hour, day, month, and year — to wipe out a third of humanity.

[16] The cavalry numbered two hundred million. I heard the count.

[17] Here's what I saw in the vision: horses, and the riders mounted on them in breastplates the color of fire, sapphire, and sulfur. The horses' heads were like lions' heads, and out of their mouths poured fire, smoke, and brimstone.

[18] By these three plagues — the fire, the smoke, the sulfur belching from their mouths — a third of humanity was killed.

[19] The horses' power was in their mouths and in their tails. Their tails were like serpents with heads, and with those heads they struck and wounded.

[20] And the rest of humanity — the ones not killed by these plagues — still refused to turn. They wouldn't let go of the work of their own hands. They kept bowing to demons and to idols of gold, silver, bronze, stone, and wood — things that can't see, can't hear, can't walk.

[21] They wouldn't repent of their murders. Or their sorceries. Or their sexual corruption. Or their thefts.

[1] Then I saw another mighty angel descending from heaven, wrapped in cloud, a rainbow crowning his head, his face like the sun, his legs like pillars of fire. [2] He held a little scroll, unrolled, in his hand. He planted his right foot on the sea, his left on the land, [3] and roared — the roar of a lion. When he roared, the seven thunders answered in their own voices.

[4] When the seven thunders spoke, I was about to write — but a voice from heaven cut me off: "Seal up what the seven thunders said. Do not write it down."

[5] Then the angel I had seen standing on sea and land lifted his right hand to heaven [6] and swore by the One who lives forever and ever, who fashioned the heavens and everything in them, the earth and everything in it, the sea and everything in it: "Time is up. [7] In the days when the seventh angel sounds his trumpet, the mystery of God will be finished — exactly as he announced to his servants, the prophets."

[8] The voice from heaven spoke to me again: "Go. Take the open scroll from the hand of the angel standing on sea and land."

[9] So I went up to the angel and asked him for the little scroll. He said, "Take it. Eat it. It will turn your stomach bitter, but in your mouth it will be sweet as honey."

[10] I took the little scroll from the angel's hand and ate it. In my mouth — sweet as honey. But once I swallowed, my stomach went bitter.

[11] Then they told me: "You must prophesy again — about many peoples, nations, languages, and kings."

[1] Then I was handed a measuring rod, like a staff, and told: "Get up. Measure God's temple, the altar, and count the worshipers there.

[2] But leave out the outer courtyard. Don't measure it. It's been handed over to the nations, and they will trample the holy city for forty-two months.

[3] And I will commission my two witnesses, and they will prophesy for 1,260 days, dressed in sackcloth."

[4] These are the two olive trees, the two lampstands, standing in the presence of the Lord of the earth.

[5] Anyone who tries to harm them — fire pours out of their mouths and devours their enemies. Anyone who wants them dead dies exactly that way.

[6] They have authority to seal the sky so no rain falls during the days of their prophecy. They have authority over the waters, to turn them to blood, and to strike the earth with any plague they choose, whenever they choose.

[7] But when they finish their testimony, the beast that climbs up out of the abyss will wage war on them, defeat them, and kill them.

[8] Their corpses will lie in the street of the great city — the city called, in spiritual code, Sodom and Egypt — the same city where their Lord was crucified.

[9] For three and a half days, people from every tribe and language and nation will stare at their bodies and refuse to let them be buried.

[10] The inhabitants of the earth will celebrate over them, throwing parties, exchanging gifts — because these two prophets had tormented everyone living on the earth.

[11] Then after three and a half days, the breath of life from God entered them. They stood up on their feet. And terror crashed down on everyone watching.

[12] Then a voice from heaven thundered, "Come up here." And they ascended into heaven inside a cloud, while their enemies watched.

[13] At that hour, a massive earthquake hit. A tenth of the city collapsed. Seven thousand people died in the quake. The survivors were terrified — and they gave glory to the God of heaven.

[14] The second disaster is over. The third is coming fast.

[15] Then the seventh angel sounded his trumpet, and loud voices erupted in heaven, saying: "The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Messiah, and he will reign forever and ever."

[16] And the twenty-four elders, seated on their thrones before God, fell facedown and worshiped him,

[17] saying: "We thank you, Lord God Almighty — the One who is and who was — because you have seized your great power and begun to reign.

[18] The nations raged, but your wrath has come. The time has come for the dead to be judged, for rewarding your servants — the prophets, the saints, all who revere your name, small and great — and for destroying those who destroy the earth."

[19] Then God's temple in heaven was thrown open, and the ark of his covenant appeared inside. Lightning, thunder, voices, an earthquake, a violent hailstorm.

[1] Then the sky tore open with a sign — staggering, impossible: a woman robed in the sun, the moon crushed beneath her feet, a crown of twelve stars blazing around her head. [2] She was pregnant. She was screaming. The labor had her, and she cried out in the agony of bringing forth.

[3] And another sign ripped across the sky — a dragon. Massive. Blood-red. Seven heads, ten horns, seven diadems gleaming on those heads. [4] His tail swept a third of the stars out of heaven and hurled them down to the earth. He crouched in front of the woman who was about to give birth, jaws open, ready to devour her child the instant it emerged.

[5] She delivered a son — a male child, the one destined to shepherd the nations with an iron scepter. And her child was snatched up to God, up to his throne. [6] The woman fled into the wilderness, to a place God himself had prepared for her, where she would be sustained for 1,260 days.

[7] Then war erupted in heaven. Michael and his angels went to battle against the dragon. The dragon and his angels fought back — [8] and lost. They were overpowered. There was no place left for them in heaven. [9] Down he came — the great dragon, the ancient serpent, the one they call the Devil and Satan, the deceiver of the whole world. Hurled to the earth. His angels hurled with him.

[10] And I heard a thunderous voice in heaven declare:
"Now it has come — the salvation, the power, the kingdom of our God, the authority of his Anointed. Because the accuser of our brothers has been thrown down — the one who stood before God night and day, accusing them. [11] They conquered him. By the blood of the Lamb. By the word of their testimony. By refusing to cling to their lives, even when death was the price. [12] So celebrate, you heavens, and everyone who dwells in them! But grief is coming for the earth and the sea — the devil has come down to you, and his fury is volcanic, because he knows his time is almost gone."

[13] When the dragon realized he had been flung to the earth, he came after the woman who had borne the son. [14] But she was given the two wings of a great eagle, to carry her into the wilderness, to her place — where she would be nourished for a time, and times, and half a time, far from the serpent's reach. [15] The serpent spewed water from his mouth like a river, chasing the woman, trying to sweep her away in the flood. [16] But the earth came to her rescue. The earth opened its mouth and swallowed the river the dragon had poured out.

[17] The dragon was livid. He turned and went to wage war against the rest of her children — those who keep God's commands and hold fast to the testimony of Jesus. [18] And he took his stand on the sand of the sea.

[1] Then I saw a beast surge up out of the sea. Ten horns, seven heads, ten crowns ringing those horns, and on every head a name that spit in God's face. [2] It looked like a leopard, but its feet were a bear's and its mouth a lion's. The dragon handed it everything — his power, his throne, sweeping authority. [3] One of its heads looked butchered, mortally wounded — but the death-wound had healed. The whole earth gawked in wonder and trailed after the beast. [4] They fell down before the dragon because he had handed his authority to the beast, and they fell down before the beast itself, chanting: "Who compares to the beast? Who could go to war against it?"

[5] It was given a mouth that spewed grand boasts and blasphemies, and it was given forty-two months to do its work. [6] So it opened that mouth against God — slandering his name, his dwelling, everyone who lives in heaven. [7] It was granted the power to make war on the holy ones and beat them. Authority was extended over every tribe, every people, every tongue, every nation. [8] Every inhabitant of the earth will worship it — everyone whose name was not written, from the foundation of the world, in the slaughtered Lamb's book of life.

[9] If you have ears, listen:

[10] If captivity is coming, into captivity you go. If the sword is coming, by the sword you die. This is where the endurance and the faith of the holy ones is forged.

[11] Then I saw another beast climbing up out of the earth. Two horns like a lamb — but when it spoke, a dragon's voice came out. [12] It exercised all the authority of the first beast in its presence, and made the earth and all who live on it bow down to that first beast — the one whose death-wound had healed. [13] It performed staggering signs, even calling fire down from heaven to earth where everyone could see. [14] By these signs — performed under the first beast's authority — it seduced the inhabitants of the earth, ordering them to build an image to the beast who had taken the sword-wound and lived. [15] It was granted power to breathe life into the image of the beast, so the image could speak — and could see to it that anyone refusing to worship it was killed. [16] It forced everyone — small and great, rich and poor, free and slave — to receive a mark on the right hand or the forehead, [17] so that no one could buy or sell without it: the mark, the name of the beast, or the number of its name.

[18] Here is where wisdom is needed. Let the one with a mind work it out — calculate the number of the beast. It is the number of a man. The number is six hundred and sixty-six.

[1] Then I looked, and there stood the Lamb on Mount Zion, and with him a hundred and forty-four thousand, his name and his Father's name written across their foreheads.

[2] And I heard a sound out of heaven — like the roar of vast waters, like the crack of thunder rolling close — and what I heard was the music of harpists striking their strings.

[3] They were singing a new song before the throne, before the four living ones and the elders. No one could learn that song except the hundred and forty-four thousand — the ones bought out of the earth.

[4] These are the ones who never defiled themselves with women; they kept themselves pure. They follow the Lamb wherever he goes. They were purchased from among humanity as the first harvest for God and for the Lamb.

[5] No lie was found in their mouths. They stand without flaw.

[6] Then I saw another messenger flying in midair, carrying the eternal good news to proclaim to every nation, tribe, tongue, and people on the ground below,

[7] calling out: "Fear God. Give him glory. The hour of his verdict has come. Bow to the One who made the sky, the land, the sea, and the springs of water."

[8] A second messenger followed, declaring: "Fallen. Fallen is Babylon the great — she who poured out for every nation the wine of her raging adultery."

[9] A third messenger followed them, his voice carrying: "Anyone who worships the beast and its image, anyone who takes its mark on the forehead or the hand —

[10] they too will drink. They will drink the wine of God's fury, poured out undiluted into the cup of his anger. They will be tortured with fire and sulfur in the presence of the holy messengers and in the presence of the Lamb.

[11] The smoke of their torment rises up forever and ever. No rest, day or night, for those who worship the beast and its image, for anyone who takes the mark of its name."

[12] Here is the endurance of the holy ones — those who hold fast to God's commands and to the faithfulness of Jesus.

[13] Then I heard a voice from heaven say: "Write this down. Blessed are the dead who die in the Lord from now on." "Yes," says the Spirit, "let them rest from their labors — their works follow with them."

[14] Then I looked, and there before me was a white cloud, and seated on the cloud one like a son of man, a golden crown on his head and a sharp sickle in his hand.

[15] Another messenger came out of the sanctuary, shouting to the one on the cloud: "Swing your sickle and reap. The hour to harvest has come. The earth's crop is ripe."

[16] So the one seated on the cloud swept his sickle across the earth, and the earth was harvested.

[17] Then another messenger came out of the sanctuary in heaven, and he too had a sharp sickle.

[18] And still another messenger came from the altar — the one with authority over fire — and he called out to the messenger with the sharp sickle: "Swing your sickle and gather the clusters from the vine of the earth. Her grapes are ripe."

[19] The messenger swung his sickle across the earth, gathered her vintage, and threw it into the great winepress of God's wrath.

[20] The press was trampled outside the city, and blood flowed from the press — rising to the height of a horse's bridle, running for sixteen hundred stadia.

[1] Then I saw another sign in the sky—massive, staggering: seven angels carrying the last seven plagues. The last, because with these God's fury runs its full course.

[2] And I saw what looked like a sea of glass shot through with fire. Standing on that sea were those who had beaten the beast—who had refused its image, refused the number of its name. They held harps God had given them,

[3] and they were singing the song of Moses, God's servant, and the song of the Lamb:

Massive, staggering—what you have done,
Lord God, Ruler of all.
Just and true—the roads you walk,
King of the nations.

[4] Who would not fear you, Lord?
Who would not give your name the glory?
You alone are holy.
Every nation will come and fall before you,
because your righteous acts now stand revealed.

[5] After this I looked, and the sanctuary opened—the tent of witness in heaven, thrown wide.

[6] Out came the seven angels carrying the seven plagues, robed in linen, pure and luminous, gold sashes wrapped across their chests.

[7] One of the four living creatures handed each angel a golden bowl, brimming with the wrath of the God who lives forever and ever.

[8] And the sanctuary filled with smoke—the weight of God's glory, the burn of his power—until no one could step inside until the seven plagues of the seven angels had run their course.

[1] Then I heard a thunderous voice from the sanctuary commanding the seven angels: "Go. Empty the seven bowls of God's wrath onto the earth."

[2] The first angel went and poured his bowl across the land. Festering, malignant sores erupted on everyone branded with the beast's mark, everyone bowing to its image.

[3] The second angel emptied his bowl into the sea. The water curdled into blood — the kind that pours from a corpse — and every living thing in the sea died.

[4] The third angel emptied his bowl into the rivers and the springs. They turned to blood.

[5] Then I heard the angel who governs the waters declare: "You are just — the One who is, who was, the Holy One — for rendering this verdict.

[6] They spilled the blood of your holy ones and your prophets, and you have given them blood to drink. They have earned it."

[7] And I heard the altar itself answer: "Yes, Lord God Almighty. Your judgments are true. Your judgments are right."

[8] The fourth angel emptied his bowl onto the sun, and the sun was given power to scorch people with fire.

[9] They burned in the furnace heat — and cursed the name of God who held authority over these plagues. They refused to repent. They refused to give him glory.

[10] The fifth angel emptied his bowl onto the throne of the beast, and its kingdom collapsed into darkness. People gnawed their tongues in agony,

[11] cursing the God of heaven for their pain and their sores. Still they would not turn from what they had done.

[12] The sixth angel emptied his bowl onto the great river Euphrates. Its waters dried up, clearing a road for the kings of the East.

[13] Then I saw three foul spirits, like frogs, leap from the mouth of the dragon, the mouth of the beast, and the mouth of the false prophet.

[14] These are demonic spirits, working signs, fanning out to the kings of the whole inhabited world to gather them for the battle on the great day of God Almighty.

[15] ("Listen — I am coming like a thief. Blessed is the one who stays awake and keeps his clothes on, who is not caught walking naked, exposed to shame.")

[16] And the spirits gathered the kings to the place called, in Hebrew, Armageddon.

[17] The seventh angel emptied his bowl into the air, and a great voice thundered from the throne in the sanctuary: "It is done."

[18] Lightning ripped the sky. Voices. Thunder. And an earthquake — violent beyond anything in human history, the worst since people first walked the earth.

[19] The great city split into three pieces. The cities of the nations collapsed into rubble. And God remembered Babylon the Great — handed her the cup brimming with the wine of his fury.

[20] Every island fled. The mountains vanished.

[21] Hailstones the weight of a man — a hundred pounds each — crashed down from the sky onto the people below. And they cursed God for the hail, because the plague was beyond bearing.

[1] Then one of the seven angels with the seven bowls walked over to me and said, "Come. I'll show you the sentence handed down to the great whore — the one perched over many waters,

[2] the one the kings of the earth bedded down with, the one whose wine of fornication left the inhabitants of the earth staggering drunk."

[3] He carried me away in the Spirit, out into a wilderness. And there she was — a woman riding a scarlet beast, a beast smothered in blasphemous names, sporting seven heads and ten horns.

[4] She was draped in purple and scarlet, dripping with gold, gemstones, and pearls. In her hand: a golden cup, brimming with obscenities — the foul liquor of her fornications.

[5] Tattooed across her forehead, a name — a riddle: BABYLON THE GREAT, MOTHER OF WHORES AND OF EVERY ABOMINATION ON EARTH.

[6] And I saw the woman: drunk. Drunk on the blood of the saints, drunk on the blood of those who bore witness to Jesus. I stared at her, dumbstruck.

[7] The angel said, "Why the shock? Let me decode her for you — the woman, and the beast she rides, the one with the seven heads and ten horns.

[8] The beast you saw — it was, and is not, and is about to climb up out of the abyss before going to its destruction. And the earth-dwellers — every name not inscribed in the book of life from the foundation of the world — will gape in wonder when they see the beast: how it was, and is not, and yet is.

[9] This calls for a mind that can see. The seven heads are seven mountains, where the woman sits enthroned. They are also seven kings —

[10] five have already fallen, one is reigning now, the other hasn't shown up yet. And when he does, his stay will be brief.

[11] The beast itself — the one that was and is not — is an eighth king, yet belongs to the seven, and is heading straight for destruction.

[12] The ten horns you saw are ten kings who haven't taken power yet, but they'll be handed authority — for one hour — alongside the beast.

[13] They share one mind. They hand over their power, their authority, to the beast.

[14] They will wage war against the Lamb — and the Lamb will crush them, because he is Lord of lords and King of kings, and those with him are the called, the chosen, the faithful."

[15] Then he said, "The waters you saw, where the whore is enthroned — those are peoples and crowds and nations and tongues.

[16] And the ten horns you saw, and the beast — they will turn on the whore. They will strip her bare, devour her flesh, and burn what's left to ash.

[17] God put it in their hearts to carry out his purpose — to share one mind, to hand their kingdom to the beast — until the words of God reach their completion.

[18] And the woman you saw is the great city that holds dominion over the kings of the earth."

[1] After this I saw another angel descending from heaven, vast in authority, and the earth itself lit up under his glory.

[2] He cried out with a voice that shook the air: "Fallen — she is fallen — Babylon the great! She has become a haunt for demons, a holding cell for every foul spirit, a cage for every unclean and detested bird.

[3] Every nation drank the wine of her raging lust. The kings of the earth bedded down with her. And the merchants of the world grew fat off the obscene scale of her appetites."

[4] Then I heard another voice from heaven: "Get out of her, my people — get out — so you take no share in her sins, no share in her plagues.

[5] Her sins have stacked up to the sky, and God has remembered every last one of her crimes.

[6] Pay her back what she paid out. Double it. Mix her cup twice as strong as the one she mixed for others.

[7] Match her self-glory and her luxury with equal torment and grief. She tells herself, 'I sit enthroned as queen, no widow's tears for me, no mourning ever.'

[8] So in a single day her plagues will land — death, grief, famine — and fire will consume her. The Lord God who judges her is strong.

[9] The kings of the earth who slept with her and shared her indulgence will weep and beat their chests when they see the smoke of her burning.

[10] They will stand at a distance, terrified of her torment, and wail: 'Disaster, disaster — the great city, Babylon the mighty city — in a single hour your judgment came.'

[11] The merchants of the earth will weep and mourn over her, because nobody is buying their cargo anymore —

[12] cargoes of gold and silver, jewels and pearls, fine linen and purple, silk and scarlet, every kind of citron wood, every object of ivory, every piece of costly wood, bronze, iron, marble;

[13] cinnamon and spice, incense and myrrh and frankincense; wine and olive oil, fine flour and wheat; cattle and sheep, horses and chariots — and bodies, and human souls.

[14] "The fruit your soul craved is gone. Every glittering thing, every luxury — vanished, never to be found again."

[15] The merchants who got rich off her will stand at a distance, terrified of her torment, weeping and mourning:

[16] "Disaster, disaster — the great city, draped in fine linen and purple and scarlet, dripping with gold and jewels and pearls —

[17] in a single hour all that wealth was laid waste." Every ship captain, every passenger and sailor, everyone who makes their living from the sea, stood at a distance

[18] and cried out as they watched the smoke of her burning: "What city was ever like the great city?"

[19] They threw dust on their heads and wailed, weeping and mourning: "Disaster, disaster — the great city, where everyone who had ships at sea grew rich off her wealth — in a single hour she was ruined."

[20] Heaven, celebrate over her! Saints and apostles and prophets, celebrate — because God has handed down your verdict against her.

[21] Then a mighty angel lifted a stone the size of a great millstone and hurled it into the sea, saying: "Like this — with this violence — Babylon the great city will be thrown down, and never found again.

[22] The sound of harpists and musicians, flute players and trumpeters — never heard in you again. No craftsman of any trade will be found in you again. The grinding of the mill — silenced.

[23] The light of a lamp will never glow in you again. The voice of bridegroom and bride — never heard in you again. Your merchants were the world's power brokers. Your sorcery seduced all the nations.

[24] And in her was found the blood of prophets and saints, and of all who have been slaughtered on the earth."

[1] After this I heard what sounded like the roar of a vast crowd in heaven, thundering: "Hallelujah! Salvation and glory and power belong to our God, [2] because his judgments are true and just. He has condemned the great prostitute who poisoned the earth with her corruption. He has avenged the blood of his servants poured out by her hand." [3] And again they thundered: "Hallelujah! The smoke of her burning rises forever and ever."

[4] Then the twenty-four elders and the four living creatures fell on their faces and worshiped God seated on the throne, saying: "Amen. Hallelujah."

[5] A voice came from the throne: "Praise our God, all his servants — everyone who fears him, small and great alike."

[6] Then I heard what sounded like a massive crowd, like the crash of mighty waters, like the boom of cracking thunder, crying out: "Hallelujah! Our Lord God, the Almighty, has begun to reign. [7] Let us rejoice and celebrate and give him the glory, because the wedding of the Lamb has come, and his bride has made herself ready. [8] She has been given fine linen to wear, bright and clean." (The fine linen is the righteous acts of God's holy ones.)

[9] Then the angel said to me, "Write this down: Blessed are those invited to the wedding feast of the Lamb." And he added, "These are the true words of God."

[10] I fell at his feet to worship him. But he said, "Don't. I'm a fellow servant — yours and your brothers' who hold to the testimony of Jesus. Worship God. The testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy."

[11] Then I saw heaven thrown open — and there was a white horse. Its rider is called Faithful and True. He judges with justice and wages war with justice. [12] His eyes blaze like fire. On his head are many crowns. A name is written on him that no one knows but himself. [13] He wears a robe soaked in blood, and his name is called: The Word of God. [14] The armies of heaven follow him, riding white horses, dressed in fine linen, white and clean. [15] From his mouth comes a sharp sword to strike down the nations. He will shepherd them with an iron rod. He himself treads the winepress of the fury of the wrath of God Almighty. [16] On his robe and on his thigh a name is written: KING OF KINGS AND LORD OF LORDS.

[17] Then I saw an angel standing in the sun. He cried out in a thundering voice to all the birds flying in midair: "Come. Gather for the great feast of God. [18] Feast on the flesh of kings, of generals, of warriors, of horses and their riders, of all people — free and slave, small and great."

[19] Then I saw the beast and the kings of the earth and their armies massed together to wage war against the rider on the horse and against his army. [20] The beast was seized — and with him the false prophet who had worked the signs on his behalf, deceiving those who had taken the mark of the beast and worshiped his image. The two of them were thrown alive into the lake of fire that burns with sulfur. [21] The rest were killed by the sword that came from the mouth of the rider on the horse, and all the birds gorged themselves on their flesh.

[1] Then I saw an angel descending from heaven, the key to the abyss in his hand and a massive chain draped over his arm.

[2] He seized the dragon — that ancient serpent, the one who is the devil, the one who is Satan — and chained him for a thousand years.

[3] He hurled him into the abyss, locked it, sealed it over him. No more deceiving the nations. Not until the thousand years had run their course. After that, he must be set loose — but only for a moment.

[4] Then I saw thrones, and the ones seated on them were given authority to judge. And I saw the souls of those who had been beheaded for bearing witness to Jesus and for the word of God — the ones who had refused to worship the beast or its image, who had refused its mark on forehead or hand. They came to life. They reigned with the Messiah for a thousand years.

[5] (The rest of the dead did not come back to life until the thousand years were finished.) This is the first resurrection.

[6] Blessed — and holy — is anyone with a share in this first resurrection. The second death has no claim on them. They will serve as priests of God and of the Messiah, and they will reign with him through the thousand years.

[7] When the thousand years end, Satan will be released from his prison

[8] and he will march out to deceive the nations spread across the four corners of the earth — Gog and Magog — gathering them for war. Their numbers will be like sand on the shore.

[9] They advanced across the breadth of the earth and surrounded the camp of the saints, the city God loves. Then fire fell from heaven and consumed them.

[10] And the devil, the one who had deceived them, was thrown into the lake of fire and sulfur, where the beast and the false prophet already were. They will be tormented day and night, forever and ever.

[11] Then I saw a great white throne, and the One seated on it. Earth and sky fled from his presence. No place could be found for them.

[12] And I saw the dead — the great and the small — standing before the throne. Books were opened. Then another book was opened: the book of life. The dead were judged by what was written in the books, judged by what they had done.

[13] The sea gave up its dead. Death and Hades gave up their dead. Each one was judged according to what they had done.

[14] Then Death and Hades themselves were hurled into the lake of fire. This is the second death — the lake of fire.

[15] And anyone whose name was not found written in the book of life was thrown into the lake of fire.

[1] Then I saw it — a new sky, a new earth. The first sky and the first earth were gone. The sea — gone too.

[2] And I saw the holy city, the new Jerusalem, descending out of heaven from God, dressed like a bride ready for her husband.

[3] And a voice thundered from the throne: "Look — God's home is now with humanity. He will live with them. They will be his people. God himself will be with them, their God.

[4] He will wipe every tear from their eyes. Death — finished. Mourning, crying, pain — gone. The old order has burned away."

[5] The One on the throne said, "I am making everything new." Then: "Write this down. These words can be trusted. They are true."

[6] He said to me, "It is done. I am the Alpha and the Omega, the beginning and the end. To anyone thirsty I will give water from the spring of life — free.

[7] Whoever conquers inherits all of this. I will be their God, and they will be my child.

[8] But the cowards, the faithless, the corrupt, murderers, the sexually depraved, sorcerers, idolaters, and every liar — their place is the lake burning with fire and sulfur. That is the second death."

[9] One of the seven angels who held the seven bowls full of the last seven plagues came over and said, "Come. I'll show you the bride, the wife of the Lamb."

[10] He carried me away in the Spirit to a great, high mountain, and showed me the holy city, Jerusalem, descending out of heaven from God,

[11] blazing with the glory of God. Her radiance was like the rarest jewel, like jasper, clear as crystal.

[12] She had a massive, high wall with twelve gates, and at the gates twelve angels. The names of the twelve tribes of Israel were carved on those gates.

[13] Three gates east, three north, three south, three west.

[14] The city's wall stood on twelve foundations, and on them were the twelve names of the twelve apostles of the Lamb.

[15] The angel speaking to me carried a gold measuring rod to measure the city, its gates, its walls.

[16] The city is a perfect square — its length equal to its width. He measured it: twelve thousand stadia. Length, width, height — all the same.

[17] He measured the wall: a hundred and forty-four cubits, by human measurement, which is also the angel's.

[18] The wall was built of jasper. The city itself — pure gold, clear as glass.

[19] The foundations of the city wall were studded with every kind of precious stone. The first foundation jasper, the second sapphire, the third agate, the fourth emerald,

[20] the fifth onyx, the sixth carnelian, the seventh chrysolite, the eighth beryl, the ninth topaz, the tenth chrysoprase, the eleventh jacinth, the twelfth amethyst.

[21] The twelve gates were twelve pearls — each gate a single pearl. And the main street of the city was pure gold, transparent as glass.

[22] I saw no temple in the city. The Lord God Almighty is its temple — he and the Lamb.

[23] The city has no need of sun or moon to shine on it. The glory of God lights it up. The Lamb is its lamp.

[24] The nations will walk in its light. The kings of the earth will bring their splendor into it.

[25] Its gates will never close — there is no night there.

[26] The glory and honor of the nations will be carried in.

[27] But nothing impure will ever enter, nothing corrupt, no liar — only those whose names are written in the Lamb's book of life.

[1] Then he showed me a river — water of life, bright as crystal, surging from the throne of God and of the Lamb,

[2] running straight down the middle of the city's main street. On either bank stood the tree of life, bearing twelve harvests, fresh fruit every month, and its leaves were medicine for the nations.

[3] Nothing accursed will exist there anymore. The throne of God and of the Lamb will be planted in the city, and his servants will worship him.

[4] They will see his face. His name will be written across their foreheads.

[5] Night is finished. They will need no lamp, no sun — because the Lord God himself will pour light over them. And they will reign forever and ever.

[6] Then he said to me: "These words are trustworthy and true. The Lord, the God who breathes through the prophets, sent his angel to show his servants what must happen — and soon.

[7] Listen — I am coming quickly. Blessed is the one who guards the words of this prophecy in this scroll."

[8] I, John, am the one who heard and saw these things. And when I had heard and seen, I dropped at the feet of the angel who had shown me, ready to worship.

[9] But he stopped me: "Don't. I am a fellow servant — yours, and your brothers the prophets, and everyone who keeps the words of this scroll. Worship God."

[10] Then he told me: "Don't seal up the words of this prophecy. The time is close.

[11] Let the unjust go on being unjust. Let the filthy stay filthy. Let the righteous keep doing right. Let the holy keep being made holy."

[12] "Look — I am coming quickly, and my reward is with me, to repay each one according to what they have done.

[13] I am the Alpha and the Omega, the First and the Last, the Beginning and the End.

[14] Blessed are those who wash their robes — they get the right to the tree of life and entry through the gates into the city.

[15] Outside: the dogs, the sorcerers, the sexually immoral, the murderers, the idolaters, and everyone who loves the lie and lives it.

[16] I, Jesus, sent my angel to deliver this testimony to you for the churches. I am the root and the offspring of David, the bright morning star."

[17] The Spirit and the Bride say: Come. Let the one who hears say: Come. Let the one who is thirsty come. Let anyone who wants it take the water of life — free.

[18] I warn everyone who hears the words of the prophecy of this scroll: if anyone adds to them, God will add to that person the plagues written in this scroll.

[19] And if anyone subtracts from the words of this scroll of prophecy, God will subtract their share in the tree of life and the holy city — both of which are written about here.

[20] The one who testifies to these things says: "Yes. I am coming quickly." Amen. Come, Lord Jesus.

[21] The grace of the Lord Jesus be with all. Amen.

[1] Paul — slave of the Messiah Jesus, summoned as an apostle, set apart for God's good news. [2] The news he promised long ago through his prophets in the sacred writings — [3] news about his Son, who came from David's bloodline in the flesh, [4] and was declared Son of God in power by the Spirit of holiness when he rose from the dead: Jesus the Messiah, our Lord. [5] Through him I received grace and apostleship to call every nation to the obedience that flows from faith, for the sake of his name — [6] and you are among them, called to belong to Jesus the Messiah.

[7] To everyone in Rome whom God loves and has called to be holy: grace to you and peace from God our Father and from the Lord Jesus the Messiah.

[8] First, I thank my God through Jesus the Messiah for all of you, because your faith is being talked about across the whole world. [9] God himself is my witness — the God I serve with my spirit in announcing the good news of his Son — that I mention you constantly, [10] always asking in my prayers that somehow, finally, by God's will, the road might open for me to come to you. [11] I'm longing to see you, to share some spiritual gift that will steady you — [12] or rather, so we can encourage each other, your faith and mine together. [13] I want you to know, brothers and sisters, that I've planned to come to you many times — and been blocked every time, until now — because I want a harvest among you as I've had among the other nations.

[14] I'm in debt to everyone — Greek and barbarian, scholar and simpleton. [15] That's why I'm so eager to bring the good news to you in Rome as well.

[16] I am not ashamed of the good news. It is the power of God for the rescue of everyone who believes — the Jew first, and also the Greek. [17] In it, God's righteousness is being unveiled, faith from start to finish, just as it stands written: *The righteous one will live by faith.*

[18] But God's wrath is being unveiled from heaven against every form of godlessness and injustice committed by people who smother the truth with their wickedness. [19] What can be known about God is plain to them — God himself has made it plain. [20] Ever since the world was made, his invisible qualities — his eternal power, his divine nature — have been clearly visible, understood through what he created. So they have no excuse. [21] They knew God. But they refused to honor him as God or to thank him. Their thinking spiraled into nonsense, and their foolish hearts went dark. [22] Claiming to be wise, they made fools of themselves, [23] swapping the glory of the immortal God for cheap imitations — images shaped like mortal humans, like birds, like four-legged beasts, like reptiles.

[24] So God let them go. He handed them over to the cravings of their own hearts, to a sexual filthiness in which they degraded their own bodies with each other. [25] They traded the truth of God for a lie. They worshiped and served the creation instead of the Creator — who is blessed forever. Amen.

[26] That's why God handed them over to disgraceful passions. Their women exchanged natural intercourse for what is unnatural. [27] In the same way, the men abandoned natural relations with women and burned with lust for each other — men committing shameless acts with men, and reaping in their own bodies the fitting payback for their delusion.

[28] And since they refused to keep God in their thinking, God handed them over to a worthless mind, to do what should never be done. [29] They are stuffed full of every kind of injustice, evil, greed, malice — packed with envy, murder, strife, deceit, spite. They are gossips, [30] slanderers, God-haters, arrogant, proud, braggarts, schemers of evil, defiant toward parents, [31] senseless, faithless, heartless, ruthless. [32] They know God's just decree — that those who do such things deserve death — and they don't just do them. They cheer on everyone else who does.

[1] So you have no defense — none of you who sit in judgment. The moment you condemn someone else, you condemn yourself, because you, the judge, are doing the exact same things. [2] And we know God's verdict on people who do such things lands squarely on the truth. [3] Do you really think — you who judge others for doing what you do yourself — that you'll slip past God's verdict? [4] Or are you trampling on the wealth of his kindness, his patience, his refusal to give up on you, blind to the fact that his kindness is meant to lead you to a change of heart?

[5] But with that stubborn, unrepentant heart of yours, you're piling up wrath for yourself — storing it up for the day when wrath breaks loose and God's righteous judgment finally goes public. [6] He will pay each person back according to what they've done. [7] To those who keep at it — pursuing glory, honor, and the life that doesn't end — he gives eternal life. [8] But for those who play politics with the truth, who chase self-interest and trade the truth for a lie, there is fury and wrath. [9] Crushing pressure and anguish for every human being who works evil — the Jew first, then the Greek. [10] But glory, honor, and peace for everyone who works good — the Jew first, then the Greek. [11] God plays no favorites.

[12] Everyone who sinned outside the law will perish outside the law. Everyone who sinned under the law will be judged by the law. [13] Hearing the law doesn't make you right with God — doing it does. [14] When Gentiles, who don't have the law, instinctively do what the law requires, they are a law to themselves, even without having one. [15] They demonstrate that the work of the law is inscribed on their hearts — their conscience joins in as witness, their own thoughts taking turns accusing or defending them. [16] All of this will surface on the day God judges the hidden secrets of every human being through Jesus the Messiah — that is the gospel I preach.

[17] But you — you call yourself a Jew. You lean on the law. You boast about God. [18] You know his will. You've been trained by the law to discern what really matters. [19] You're confident you're a guide for the blind, a light for those stumbling in the dark, [20] an instructor of fools, a teacher of the immature — because in the law you've got knowledge and truth in concrete form. [21] So then — you who teach others — don't you teach yourself? You preach against stealing. Do you steal? [22] You forbid adultery. Do you commit adultery? You loathe idols. Do you rob temples? [23] You brag about the law — and then dishonor God by breaking it? [24] As Scripture says: *Because of you, God's name gets dragged through the mud among the nations.*

[25] Circumcision matters — if you keep the law. But the moment you break the law, your circumcision has already become uncircumcision. [26] And if an uncircumcised man keeps the law's requirements, won't his uncircumcision be counted as circumcision? [27] The physically uncircumcised person who fulfills the law will stand as judge over you — you with your written code and your circumcision, who break it. [28] Being a Jew isn't an outward thing. Circumcision isn't something done to the flesh. [29] The real Jew is the one on the inside. Real circumcision happens in the heart — by the Spirit, not by the letter. That person's praise doesn't come from people. It comes from God.

[1] So what advantage does the Jew have? What value is there in circumcision? [2] Enormous, in every way. First and foremost: they were entrusted with the very words of God. [3] So what if some proved unfaithful? Does their faithlessness cancel God's faithfulness? [4] Not a chance. Let God be true even if every human being turns out to be a liar. As it stands written: "So that you'll be vindicated in your words and prevail when you're judged."

[5] But if our wrongdoing actually highlights God's rightness, what do we say? Is God unjust to bring down his wrath? (I'm speaking in human terms here.) [6] Absolutely not. If that were true, how could God judge the world at all? [7] But if my lie makes God's truth shine brighter to his glory, why am I still being condemned as a sinner? [8] And why not just say — as some slanderously claim we say — "Let's do evil so good can come of it"? Their condemnation is well-deserved.

[9] So where does that leave us? Are we Jews better off? Not at all. We've already laid out the charge: everyone, Jew and Gentile alike, is under sin's power. [10] As the Scriptures put it:

"No one is righteous — not a single one.
[11] No one understands.
No one is searching for God.
[12] Everyone has wandered off,
together they've gone bad.
No one does good —
not even one.
[13] Their throats are open graves.
Their tongues spin deceit.
The venom of vipers sits behind their lips.
[14] Their mouths overflow with curses and bitterness.
[15] Their feet are quick to spill blood.
[16] Ruin and misery mark their trail.
[17] The way of peace — they've never known it.
[18] There is no fear of God before their eyes."

[19] Now we know that whatever the Law says, it says to those under the Law — so that every mouth is silenced and the whole world stands accountable before God. [20] No one will be declared righteous in his sight by keeping the Law. What the Law does is show us what sin looks like.

[21] But now — apart from the Law entirely — God's righteousness has broken into view, testified to by the Law and the Prophets all along. [22] It's God's righteousness, made available through faith in Jesus Christ, to everyone who believes. No distinctions. [23] Because everyone has sinned. Everyone falls short of God's glory. [24] And everyone is justified — declared right — as a gift, by his grace, through the rescue that came in Christ Jesus. [25] God put him forward as the place where mercy meets blood — accessed by faith. This was to demonstrate God's justice, because in his forbearance he had passed over former sins. [26] It was to demonstrate his justice in the present moment — proving that God himself is just, and the one who makes righteous everyone who lives by faith in Jesus.

[27] So where's the room for boasting? It's been shut out. By what principle? Performance? No — by the principle of faith. [28] Because we conclude that a person is made right with God by faith, apart from doing the Law. [29] Or is God the God of Jews only? Isn't he the God of Gentiles too? Yes, of Gentiles too — [30] since God is one. He'll justify the circumcised by faith and the uncircumcised through that same faith. [31] So are we tearing down the Law by all this faith talk? Far from it. We're actually establishing it.

[1] So what do we say about Abraham, our ancestor by blood? What did he actually discover?

[2] If Abraham earned his standing with God by what he did, he'd have something to brag about — but not in front of God.

[3] What does Scripture say? "Abraham trusted God, and that trust was credited to him as righteousness."

[4] Now when someone works a job, the paycheck isn't a gift — it's owed. It's wages.

[5] But for the one who doesn't work and instead trusts the God who declares the godless righteous — that trust gets credited as righteousness.

[6] David sings the same song when he describes the blessing on the person God counts righteous apart from anything they've done:

[7] "How blessed are those whose lawbreaking is forgiven, whose sins are buried out of sight.

[8] How blessed is the one whose sin the Lord refuses to count against them."

[9] Now — is this blessing reserved for the circumcised, or does it reach the uncircumcised too? We just said: Abraham's trust was credited to him as righteousness.

[10] Under what circumstances was it credited? After he was circumcised, or before? Not after. Before.

[11] He received circumcision later as a sign — a seal stamping the righteousness he already had through trust while still uncircumcised. So Abraham becomes father to every uncircumcised person who trusts, and righteousness gets credited to them too.

[12] And he's also father to the circumcised — not to those who merely carry the mark, but to those who walk in the same trusting footsteps Abraham walked before he was ever circumcised.

[13] The promise to Abraham and his offspring — that he would inherit the world — didn't come through keeping the law. It came through the righteousness of trust.

[14] Because if the law-keepers are the heirs, then trust is hollowed out and the promise is dead on arrival.

[15] The law produces wrath. Where there's no law, there's no violation.

[16] That's why it all hinges on trust — so the promise can be sheer gift, guaranteed to every descendant of Abraham. Not just those under the law, but everyone who shares Abraham's trust. He's father to us all.

[17] As Scripture says: "I have made you father of many nations." He's our father in the eyes of the God he trusted — the God who breathes life into the dead and speaks reality into being out of nothing.

[18] When every hope was gone, Abraham hoped anyway, and trusted, and so became father of many nations, exactly as he had been told: "That's how numerous your offspring will be."

[19] His trust didn't flinch — even when he looked at his own body, basically dead at a hundred years old, even when he considered Sarah's womb, long shut down.

[20] He didn't waver at God's promise through unbelief. He grew stronger in trust, giving God the glory,

[21] absolutely convinced that God could deliver on what he had promised.

[22] That's why it was "credited to him as righteousness."

[23] But these words — "credited to him" — weren't written just for Abraham.

[24] They were written for us too. The same crediting is coming our way — we who trust the One who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead.

[25] He was handed over for our failures and raised to set us right.

[1] So then — since we've been declared right with God through faith, we have peace with him through our Lord Jesus the Messiah. [2] He's the one who opened the door, and through him we now stand in this grace where we live. And we celebrate — full of confidence — the hope of sharing God's own glory.

[3] More than that: we celebrate even in the middle of suffering. Because we know what suffering produces — endurance. [4] And endurance produces character. And character produces hope. [5] And hope doesn't leave us looking like fools, because God's love has been poured out — flooded — into our hearts through the Holy Spirit he gave us.

[6] Think about it: while we were still powerless, still unable to save ourselves, at exactly the right moment, the Messiah died for people who had no use for God. [7] You can barely imagine someone dying for a righteous person. Maybe — just maybe — for someone genuinely good, someone might find the courage to die. [8] But God proves his love for us like this: while we were still in the middle of our rebellion, the Messiah died for us.

[9] So if his blood was enough to set us right with God, how much more will he rescue us from the wrath that's coming? [10] If we were reconciled to God through his Son's death while we were still his enemies — how much more, now that we're reconciled, will we be saved by his life? [11] And not just saved — we celebrate in God himself, through our Lord Jesus the Messiah, the one who brought us back.

[12] Here's the picture: through one man, sin entered the world. And through sin came death. And death spread to every human being, because every human being sinned. [13] Sin was in the world before the Law arrived — but sin isn't held against anyone when there's no law to define it. [14] Still, death reigned — from Adam to Moses — even over people whose sin didn't look like Adam's deliberate breach. Adam was a preview. A pattern of the one who was coming.

[15] But the gift is nothing like the trespass. If one man's failure brought death to the many — how much more has God's grace, and the gift that came through the grace of one man, Jesus the Messiah, overflowed to the many? [16] And the gift isn't like the consequence of that one man's sin. The verdict after one sin brought condemnation. The gift, after countless sins, brings acquittal. [17] If death set up its kingdom through one man's trespass — how much more will those who receive the flood of grace and the gift of righteousness reign in life through the one man, Jesus the Messiah?

[18] So then: one trespass led to condemnation for everyone. One act of righteousness leads to life-giving acquittal for everyone. [19] Through one man's disobedience, the many were made sinners. Through one man's obedience, the many will be made righteous.

[20] The Law slipped in to make the trespass visible — to multiply it. But where sin multiplied, grace went into overdrive. [21] So that just as sin reigned in death, grace would reign through righteousness, bringing eternal life through Jesus the Messiah our Lord.

[1] So what do we do with this? Keep sinning so grace has more to do? [2] Absolutely not. We died to sin — how could we keep living in it? [3] Or have you forgotten? Every one of us who was baptized into Christ Jesus was baptized into his death. [4] We were buried with him through that baptism — buried into death itself — so that just as Christ was raised from the dead by the Father's radiant power, we too would walk in a new kind of life.

[5] If we've been grafted into the likeness of his death, we will absolutely share in the likeness of his resurrection. [6] Understand this: the old self was nailed to that cross with him. The body that sin used as its instrument was dismantled. We are no longer enslaved to it. [7] A dead person is finished with sin's claims. [8] And if we died with Christ, we believe we will also live with him — [9] because we know what's true: Christ, raised from the dead, doesn't die again. Death has no more grip on him. [10] The death he died, he died to sin once — finished, done. The life he lives, he lives to God.

[11] So count yourselves the same way: dead to sin, alive to God in Christ Jesus.

[12] Don't let sin rule in your mortal body. Don't obey its cravings. [13] Stop handing over the parts of yourself as weapons in sin's war. Instead, offer yourselves to God — people brought back from the dead — and give every part of yourself to him as instruments of what is right. [14] Sin will not be your master, because you are not under law. You are under grace.

[15] So then — should we sin because we're not under law but under grace? Absolutely not. [16] Don't you see? Whatever you surrender yourself to obey, that's what owns you. Either sin, which ends in death — or obedience, which leads to righteousness. [17] But thank God: you used to be slaves of sin, but you gave your whole heart to the pattern of teaching you were handed. [18] Set free from sin, you became slaves of righteousness.

[19] I'm speaking in human terms here, because of the weakness of your flesh. Just as you once gave your body parts over to impurity and lawlessness — which only bred more lawlessness — now give those same parts over to righteousness, which leads to holiness.

[20] When sin owned you, righteousness had no claim on you. [21] And what did you get out of it? Things you're now ashamed to name. They all end at the same place: death. [22] But now — freed from sin, bound to God — the harvest is holiness, and the end of the road is life that doesn't end. [23] Sin pays out in death. God's free gift is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.

[1] Are you tracking with me, brothers and sisters? I'm talking to people who know how law works — and law only has jurisdiction over a person while they're alive.

[2] Take a married woman. The law binds her to her husband as long as he's breathing. The moment he dies, that whole legal apparatus around the marriage collapses.

[3] So if she takes up with another man while her husband is alive, she's an adulteress. Plain and simple. But once her husband dies, she's free of that law. She can marry someone else and there's nothing adulterous about it.

[4] Same logic, my brothers and sisters. You died to the law through the body of Christ. Why? So you could belong to someone else — to the one raised from the dead. So that we'd actually bear fruit for God.

[5] Back when we were running on the flesh, our sinful passions — the ones the law lit up like neon signs — were at work in every limb and nerve, producing fruit for death.

[6] But now we've been sprung from the law. We died to what held us captive. So we serve in the new way of the Spirit, not the old way of the written code.

[7] So what am I saying? That the law itself is sin? Absolutely not. But I would never have recognized sin without the law. I wouldn't have known what coveting was if the law hadn't said, "Don't covet."

[8] And sin — seizing its opportunity through that very commandment — manufactured in me every kind of craving. Apart from law, sin is a corpse.

[9] There was a time I was alive without the law. Then the commandment showed up. Sin came roaring to life —

[10] and I dropped dead. The very commandment meant to give life turned out to deal me death.

[11] Sin grabbed the commandment like a weapon, ambushed me, and killed me with it.

[12] So the law itself is holy. The commandment is holy and righteous and good.

[13] Then did something good become death to me? No. Sin did. Sin used what was good to kill me — so that sin would be exposed for what it is, so that through the commandment it would be shown up as monstrous beyond words.

[14] We know the law is spiritual. I'm the problem — I'm flesh, sold off to sin like chattel.

[15] I don't even understand what I do. The things I want to do, I don't. The things I hate — those are what I end up doing.

[16] And if I'm doing what I don't want, then I'm agreeing with the law that the law is good.

[17] Which means it's no longer really me pulling the strings — it's sin living inside me.

[18] I know nothing good lives in me — that is, in my flesh. The wanting is there. The doing isn't.

[19] The good I want to do, I don't do. The evil I don't want — that's what I keep doing.

[20] And if I do the very thing I don't want, then I'm not the one doing it. It's sin, squatting inside me.

[21] So I've discovered this principle: when I want to do good, evil is right there at my elbow.

[22] On the inside, in my deepest self, I delight in God's law.

[23] But I see another law running through my body — waging war against the law in my mind, dragging me off as a prisoner to the law of sin running loose in my limbs.

[24] What a wretched man I am. Who is going to rescue me from this body of death?

[25] Thank God — through Jesus Christ our Lord! So here's where I land: with my mind I serve God's law. With my flesh, sin's law.

[1] So here it is: no condemnation. None. Not for those who belong to the Anointed One, Jesus. [2] Because in him, the law of the Spirit — the Spirit who breathes life — has cut you loose from the old law, the one chained to sin and death. [3] What the law could never pull off — strangled as it was by our weakness — God did. He sent his own Son, wearing flesh that looked exactly like our sin-warped flesh, and sent him on account of sin. Right there, in that flesh, God passed sentence on sin itself. [4] He did this so the just verdict the law was always reaching for would finally be fulfilled in us — in those of us who no longer march to the drum of the flesh but step in time with the Spirit.

[5] People living by the flesh have their minds tuned to its frequency. People living by the Spirit are tuned to his. [6] The flesh's mindset is a dead end — literally, death. The Spirit's mindset is life and peace, deep and unshakable. [7] Because the flesh's mindset is hostility toward God. It refuses to bend to his law. It can't. [8] Anyone stuck in the flesh cannot please God. Period.

[9] But you — you are not in the flesh. You are in the Spirit, assuming God's Spirit actually lives in you. And if anyone does not have the Spirit of the Anointed, they do not belong to him. [10] But if the Anointed is in you, then yes — your body is a dead man walking because of sin, but your spirit is alive, surging with life, because of righteousness. [11] And if the Spirit of the One who raised Jesus from the dead lives in you, then the One who raised the Anointed from the dead will also bring your mortal bodies roaring back to life through his Spirit who lives in you.

[12] So, brothers and sisters — we owe the flesh nothing. We do not have to live the way it demands. [13] Live by the flesh and you will die. But put the body's schemes to death by the Spirit — and you will live.

[14] Everyone the Spirit of God leads is a son or daughter of God. [15] You did not receive a spirit that drags you back into slavery and fear. You received the Spirit of adoption — and through him we cry out, *Abba* — *Father*. [16] The Spirit himself testifies, right alongside our own spirit, that we are God's children. [17] And if children, then heirs — heirs of God, co-heirs with the Anointed. Provided we suffer with him. So that we may also be glorified with him.

[18] Here's what I'm convinced of: whatever we suffer right now does not even register on the scale next to the glory about to break over us. [19] Creation itself is on tiptoe, craning its neck, desperate for the moment God's children are finally revealed. [20] Creation was dragged into futility — not by choice, but by the One who subjected it — and even that came with hope: [21] hope that creation itself will be sprung loose from its bondage to decay and brought into the wide-open freedom of the glory belonging to God's children. [22] We know the whole creation has been groaning, contracting in labor pains, right up to this moment. [23] And not only creation. We do it too — we who have the firstfruits of the Spirit. We groan inside ourselves, aching for adoption to be complete: the redemption of our bodies. [24] This hope is what saved us. But hope you can already see isn't hope — who hopes for what's already in hand? [25] If we're hoping for what we can't yet see, we wait for it with stubborn endurance.

[26] And the Spirit comes alongside us in our weakness. We don't even know what to pray for — but the Spirit himself intercedes for us with groans too deep for words. [27] And the One who searches hearts knows exactly what the Spirit means, because the Spirit pleads for God's people in step with God's own will.

[28] And we know this: for those who love God, for those called according to his purpose, God is weaving everything together for good. [29] Because those he knew from the beginning, he also marked out beforehand to be shaped into the image of his Son — so that the Son would be the firstborn among a vast family of brothers and sisters. [30] And those he marked out, he called. Those he called, he set right. Those he set right, he glorified.

[31] So what do we say to all this? If God is for us — who can stand against us? [32] He did not spare his own Son. He handed him over for us all. How will he not, along with him, freely give us everything? [33] Who will dare bring a charge against God's chosen ones? God is the one who acquits. [34] Who is going to condemn us? The Anointed Jesus is the one who died — more than that, was raised — and is now at God's right hand, pleading our case.

[35] Who will separate us from the love of the Anointed? Pressure? Anguish? Persecution? Hunger? Nakedness? Danger? A sword? [36] As it stands written: *For your sake we are being killed all day long; we are counted as sheep marked for slaughter.* [37] No — in all these things we are crushing it, more than conquerors, through the One who loved us. [38] Because I am convinced — utterly — that neither death nor life, neither angels nor rulers, neither what is here nor what is coming, neither any power, [39] neither height nor depth, nor anything else in all creation — nothing — will be able to separate us from the love of God in the Anointed Jesus, our Lord.

[1] I'm telling you the truth — I speak in the Messiah, I'm not lying, my conscience confirms it in the Holy Spirit — [2] there's a massive grief in me, a pain that won't let go. [3] I would trade my own salvation, be cut off from the Messiah myself, if it could save them — my own people, my flesh and blood, [4] the Israelites. They got everything: the adoption, the glory, the covenants, the law, the worship, the promises. [5] The patriarchs are theirs. The Messiah Himself, in His humanity, came from them — He who is over all, God forever blessed. Amen.

[6] But don't think God's word has collapsed. Because not everyone descended from Israel is actually Israel. [7] Being Abraham's biological children doesn't make you his real children. The line was drawn explicitly: "Your offspring will be reckoned through Isaac." [8] Translation: it's not the children of the flesh who are God's children — it's the children of the promise who count as offspring. [9] And here's the promise: "I'll come back at the appointed time, and Sarah will have a son."
