[10] And it's not just that. Look at Rebecca, pregnant with twins by one man, our father Isaac. [11] Before the boys were even born, before either had done anything good or bad — so that God's purpose by election would stand, [12] depending on the One who calls, not on human achievement — she was told, "The older will serve the younger." [13] As it's written: "Jacob I loved. Esau I rejected."

[14] So what do we say? Is God unjust? Absolutely not. [15] He says to Moses: "I'll have mercy on whoever I have mercy on. I'll show compassion to whoever I show compassion to." [16] So it doesn't depend on human will or human striving — it depends on God's mercy. [17] Scripture says it to Pharaoh: "I raised you up for exactly this purpose — to display my power through you, to make my name known across the whole earth." [18] So He has mercy on whom He chooses and hardens whom He chooses.

[19] You'll say to me: "Then why does He still find fault? Who can resist His will?" [20] Who do you think you are, human, to talk back to God? Does the thing molded say to the molder, "Why did you make me like this?" [21] Doesn't the potter have the right over the clay — to make one vessel for honor and another for ordinary use, from the same lump?

[22] What if God, wanting to show His wrath and reveal His power, endured with enormous patience vessels of wrath already prepared for destruction — [23] precisely so He could make known the riches of His glory on vessels of mercy, which He prepared beforehand for glory, [24] namely us, the ones He called, not only from the Jews but also from the Gentiles?

[25] As He says in Hosea:

I'll call them "my people" who weren't my people,
and "beloved" the one who wasn't loved.
[26] And in the very place where it was said to them, "You're not my people,"
there they'll be called sons of the living God.

[27] Isaiah cries out about Israel:

Though the children of Israel be like the sand of the sea,
only a remnant will be saved.
[28] For the Lord will execute His sentence on the earth —
finishing it, cutting it short.

[29] And as Isaiah said earlier:

If the Lord of armies hadn't left us offspring,
we would have become Sodom,
we would have looked like Gomorrah.

[30] So what's the conclusion? Gentiles, who weren't even chasing righteousness, caught it — a righteousness that comes through faith. [31] But Israel, pursuing a law of righteousness, never reached that law. [32] Why not? Because they didn't pursue it by faith, but as if it could be earned. They tripped over the stumbling stone, [33] just as it's written:

Look — I'm placing a stone in Zion that makes people stumble,
a rock that trips them up.
But whoever trusts in Him won't be put to shame.

[1] Brothers and sisters, what my heart aches for, what I beg God for on their behalf, is their rescue. [2] I'll vouch for them on this: they burn with zeal for God. But the fire isn't fueled by knowledge. [3] Blind to the righteousness God gives, scrambling to build their own, they refused to bow to the righteousness only God provides. [4] Because Christ is the finish line of the law — righteousness, delivered, for everyone who trusts him.

[5] Moses described the righteousness that comes through the law like this: the one who pulls it off lives by it. [6] But the righteousness that comes through faith speaks differently: Don't whisper to yourself, "Who will climb up to heaven?" — as if Christ needed dragging down. [7] Or, "Who will plunge into the abyss?" — as if Christ needed hauling up from the dead. [8] No — listen to what it actually says: "The word is right here. On your lips. In your heart." That word is the message of faith we proclaim.

[9] Here it is: if your mouth confesses Jesus as Lord and your heart trusts that God raised him from the dead — you will be saved. [10] The heart trusts, and righteousness follows. The mouth confesses, and salvation breaks through. [11] Scripture has been saying it all along: anyone who trusts him will never be put to shame. [12] No distinction. Jew, Greek — same Lord over all, pouring out riches on everyone who calls. [13] Because everyone — everyone — who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved.

[14] But how can they call on someone they haven't trusted? How can they trust someone they haven't heard of? How can they hear without someone announcing it? [15] And how can anyone announce it unless they're sent? As it's written: "How beautiful — the feet of those carrying good news."

[16] But not everyone has listened. Isaiah was already lamenting it: "Lord, who has believed our report?" [17] So faith comes from hearing, and hearing comes through the word about Christ. [18] But did they not hear? Of course they did. "Their voice has gone out across the earth, their words to the ends of the world."

[19] Then did Israel not understand? Moses said it first: "I'll make you jealous through a non-nation. I'll provoke you with a people who don't get it." [20] And Isaiah dares to say it outright: "I was found by those who weren't looking. I showed myself to those who never asked." [21] But to Israel he says: "All day long I stretched out my hands to a people who refuse and resist."

[1] So I ask: has God thrown his people overboard? Absolutely not. I'm an Israelite myself, descended from Abraham, from the tribe of Benjamin. [2] God has not rejected the people he knew and chose from the start. Don't you remember what Scripture says about Elijah — how he made his case against Israel before God?

[3] "Lord, they've killed your prophets, torn down your altars. I'm the only one left, and now they want my life too."

[4] And what was God's reply? "I have kept seven thousand for myself — men who have never bent the knee to Baal."

[5] It's the same right now. There's a remnant — chosen by grace. [6] And if grace is the basis, then it has nothing to do with works. Otherwise grace stops being grace.

[7] So here's where we land: what Israel was chasing, Israel did not catch. The chosen ones caught it. The rest were hardened — [8] just as Scripture says:

God gave them a spirit of stupor,
eyes that don't see,
ears that don't hear —
right down to this day.

[9] And David puts it this way:

Let their table become a trap, a snare,
a stumbling block, the payment they deserve.
[10] Let their eyes go dark so they cannot see.
Let their backs be bent forever.

[11] So I ask again: did they stumble in order to fall flat — finished? Not a chance. Their stumble opened the door for salvation to reach the nations, which was meant to make Israel jealous. [12] And if their stumble means riches for the world, if their loss means wealth for the nations — imagine what their full restoration will bring.

[13] Now I'm talking to you Gentiles. As the apostle sent to the nations, I make much of this calling — [14] hoping somehow to provoke my own people to jealousy and save some of them. [15] Because if their rejection means the world gets reconciled to God, what will their acceptance be but life rising from the dead?

[16] If the first handful of dough is holy, the whole batch is holy. If the root is holy, so are the branches.

[17] Now — some of the branches were broken off, and you, a wild olive shoot, were grafted in among the rest. You're sharing in the rich sap from the root. [18] So don't strut over those broken branches. If you're tempted to, remember: you don't support the root. The root supports you.

[19] You'll say, "Branches were broken off so I could be grafted in."

[20] Fine. But they were broken off because of unbelief, and you stand only because of faith. So drop the arrogance. Have some fear. [21] If God didn't spare the natural branches, he won't spare you either.

[22] Take a hard look, then, at the kindness and the severity of God: severity toward those who fell, kindness toward you — provided you stay in his kindness. Otherwise you'll be cut off too.

[23] And those who were cut off — if they don't stay in unbelief, they'll be grafted right back in. God has the power to do it. [24] If you were cut from a wild olive tree, against nature, and grafted into a cultivated one — how much more easily will the natural branches be grafted back into their own tree?

[25] I don't want you missing this, brothers and sisters — I don't want you thinking you've got it all figured out: a hardening has come over part of Israel until the full number of the nations comes in. [26] And then — all Israel will be saved. As Scripture says:

The Deliverer will come from Zion;
he will sweep godlessness away from Jacob.
[27] And this is my covenant with them
when I take away their sins.

[28] In terms of the gospel, they are enemies — for your sake. In terms of God's choosing, they are loved — for the sake of their ancestors. [29] Because God's gifts and his call are never taken back.

[30] Once, you were the disobedient ones. Now, through their disobedience, you've received mercy. [31] In the same way, they've been disobedient now — so that, through the mercy shown to you, they too may receive mercy. [32] God locked everyone up in disobedience so he could pour mercy out on everyone.

[33] Oh, the depth — the riches, the wisdom, the knowledge of God!
How unsearchable his judgments,
how untraceable his ways.

[34] Who has ever known the mind of the Lord?
Who has ever advised him?
[35] Who has ever handed him anything
and put God in their debt?

[36] Because everything comes from him.
Everything moves through him.
Everything bends back toward him.

To him be the glory forever. Amen.

[1] So here's what I'm asking you, brothers and sisters—and I'm asking it on the strength of everything God's mercy has done for you: take your body, your actual physical life, and place it on the altar. A living sacrifice. Holy. The kind of offering God actually wants. This is what real worship looks like—worship with your mind switched on.

[2] Stop letting this age press you into its mold. Be transformed instead—from the inside out, by a mind made new. Then you'll be able to discern what God actually wants: what's good, what pleases him, what hits the mark perfectly.

[3] By the grace given to me, I'm telling every single one of you: don't think of yourself as more than you are. Think with sober judgment. Measure yourself by the faith God has handed you—nothing more, nothing less.

[4] Think of it like a body. One body, many parts, and each part has its own job to do.

[5] That's us. Many of us, but one body in Christ—and each of us belongs to all the others.

[6] We've been given different gifts, shaped by the grace poured into us. If your gift is prophecy, prophesy in proportion to your faith.

[7] If it's service, serve. If it's teaching, teach.

[8] If it's encouragement, encourage. If you give, give generously. If you lead, lead with intensity. If you show mercy, do it with joy.

[9] Let love be the real thing—no masks, no performance. Hate what is evil. Cling to what is good with both hands.

[10] Love each other like family. Outdo one another in showing honor.

[11] Don't drift into spiritual laziness. Keep the fire burning. Serve the Lord with everything you've got.

[12] Let hope make you celebrate. Let suffering make you steady. Let prayer be constant—a current running through everything.

[13] When God's people are in need, step in. Make hospitality your reflex.

[14] Bless the people who persecute you. Bless them—do not curse them.

[15] When others are celebrating, celebrate with them. When they're grieving, grieve with them.

[16] Live in harmony with each other. Don't get above yourself—stay close to the people the world overlooks. Don't be impressed with your own wisdom.

[17] Don't repay evil with evil. Aim for what everyone can recognize as good.

[18] As much as it depends on you, live at peace with everyone.

[19] Don't take revenge, friends. Leave room for God's wrath. Scripture says, "Vengeance is mine. I'll handle it," says the Lord.

[20] Try this instead: if your enemy is hungry, feed him. If he's thirsty, give him a drink. Doing that piles burning coals on his head.

[21] Don't let evil defeat you. Defeat evil with good.

[1] Submit to the governing authorities. Every authority that exists exists because God placed it there. The structures of power did not arise on their own — God arranged them.

[2] So when you defy authority, you are defying what God set in place, and those who do will face the consequences they have earned.

[3] Rulers are not a threat to those doing good — they are a threat to those doing evil. You want to live without fear of the authorities? Do what is right. You will get their approval, not their wrath.

[4] The one in power is God's servant working for your good. But if you cross the line into evil, then fear — because the sword in their hand is not decorative. They are God's servant, an agent of justice executing wrath on the wrongdoer.

[5] So submit — not just because you are afraid of getting punished, but because your conscience demands it.

[6] This is also why you pay taxes. The officials are God's servants, devoting themselves to exactly this work.

[7] Give everyone what you owe them: taxes to the tax collector, tariffs to the tariff officer, respect to those who deserve respect, honor to those who deserve honor.

[8] Owe nothing to anyone — except love. That debt never gets paid off. And whoever loves another has already fulfilled the law.

[9] Every command — do not commit adultery, do not murder, do not steal, do not covet, and all the rest — gets summed up in one line: love your neighbor as yourself.

[10] Love does no harm to a neighbor. That is why love is the law fulfilled.

[11] And you know what time it is. The hour has come to wake up. Our rescue is closer now than when we first believed.

[12] The night is almost gone. Daybreak is here. So strip off the works of darkness and put on the armor of light.

[13] Walk like people who live in the daylight — not in wild parties and drunken stupors, not in beds of indulgence and shameless lust, not in fights and jealous rages.

[14] Instead, clothe yourself in the Lord Jesus Christ. And stop scheming ways to feed the cravings of the flesh.

[1] Welcome the one whose faith is fragile — but not to argue them into the ground over what they believe. [2] One person is convinced they can eat anything; another, whose faith is shaky, sticks to vegetables. [3] The eater must not look down on the abstainer, and the abstainer must not pass judgment on the eater — because God has welcomed them both. [4] Who do you think you are, judging someone else's servant? Whether they stand or fall is their Master's business. And they will stand — because the Lord has the power to hold them up.

[5] One person treats certain days as sacred; another treats every day the same. Let each be fully convinced in their own mind. [6] The one who honors the day does it for the Lord. The one who eats does it for the Lord — they thank God. The one who abstains does it for the Lord too — and gives thanks just the same. [7] None of us lives for ourselves alone, and none of us dies for ourselves alone. [8] If we live, we live for the Lord. If we die, we die for the Lord. Living or dying — we belong to him. [9] This is exactly why Christ died and rose again: so he could be Lord of both the dead and the living.

[10] So why are you sitting in judgment over your brother? Why do you sneer at your sister? Every one of us will stand before the judgment seat of God. [11] As Scripture says: *As surely as I live, says the Lord, every knee will bend before me, and every tongue will confess to God.* [12] Each of us, then, will give an account of ourselves to God.

[13] So let's stop putting each other on trial. Instead, make this your verdict: never lay down a stumbling block in your brother's path. [14] I know — I'm convinced in the Lord Jesus — that nothing is unclean in itself. But if someone believes it's unclean, then for them, it is. [15] If your eating wounds your brother, you are no longer walking in love. Do not let your food destroy someone Christ died for. [16] Do not let what you consider good be slandered as evil. [17] The kingdom of God is not about food and drink. It's righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit. [18] Serve Christ this way, and you please God — and people see it.

[19] So chase after what makes for peace, and what builds each other up. [20] Do not tear down God's work over a plate of food. Everything is clean — but it becomes wrong when what you eat makes someone else stumble. [21] Better to skip the meat, skip the wine, skip anything that trips your brother up. [22] Whatever you believe about these things, keep it between you and God. Blessed is the one who doesn't condemn themselves over what they've decided is fine. [23] But the one who has doubts and eats anyway is already condemned, because they're not acting from faith. And anything that doesn't come from faith is sin.

[1] Those of us who are strong have an obligation — to carry the weaknesses of those who aren't, and not just please ourselves. [2] Each of us should aim to please our neighbor, for their good, to build them up. [3] Even the Messiah didn't please himself. As it stands written: "The insults of those who insulted you fell on me." [4] Everything written in earlier times was written to teach us — so that through endurance and the encouragement of the Scriptures we might hold onto hope. [5] May the God who supplies endurance and encouragement give you the same mindset toward one another that flows from Christ Jesus, [6] so that together, with one voice, you would glorify the God and Father of our Lord Jesus the Messiah.

[7] So welcome one another, then — just as Christ welcomed you, to the glory of God. [8] What I'm saying is this: Christ became a servant to the circumcised on behalf of God's truthfulness, to confirm the promises made to the patriarchs, [9] and so that the nations would glorify God for his mercy. As it stands written: "Therefore I will praise you among the nations, and sing to your name." [10] And again: "Rejoice, you nations, with his people." [11] And again: "Praise the Lord, all you nations; let all the peoples sing his praise." [12] And again, Isaiah says: "The root of Jesse will rise — he who rises to rule the nations. In him the nations will set their hope." [13] May the God of hope fill you with all joy and peace as you trust him, so that you overflow with hope by the power of the Holy Spirit.

[14] I'm convinced about you, my brothers and sisters — convinced that you yourselves are full of goodness, packed with knowledge, fully capable of instructing one another. [15] Still, I've written to you boldly on certain points, as a reminder, because of the grace God gave me [16] to be a minister of Christ Jesus to the nations — serving as a priest of God's good news, so that the offering of the nations might be acceptable, made holy by the Holy Spirit.

[17] So in Christ Jesus I have something to boast about in my work for God. [18] I won't presume to speak of anything except what Christ has accomplished through me to bring the nations to obedience — by word and deed, [19] by the power of signs and wonders, by the power of the Spirit of God. The result: from Jerusalem all the way around to Illyricum, I have fully delivered the gospel of Christ. [20] My ambition has always been to preach where Christ's name had never been spoken — I wouldn't build on someone else's foundation. [21] As it stands written: "Those who were never told of him will see, and those who never heard will understand."

[22] That's why I've been kept from coming to you so many times. [23] But now I'm out of room in these regions, and for years I've longed to see you — [24] so I plan to do it on my way to Spain. I hope to see you as I pass through, and to be sent on my way by you after I've enjoyed your company for a while. [25] Right now, though, I'm headed to Jerusalem to serve the saints there. [26] Macedonia and Achaia were pleased to make a contribution for the poor among the saints in Jerusalem. [27] Pleased to do it — and they owed it. If the nations have shared in the spiritual blessings of the Jews, they owe it to serve them in material things. [28] So once I've finished this task and sealed this offering safely to them, I'll set out for Spain by way of you. [29] And I know that when I come to you, I'll come in the full blessing of Christ.

[30] I'm begging you, brothers and sisters — by our Lord Jesus Christ and by the love of the Spirit — join me in the struggle, by praying to God for me. [31] Pray that I'll be rescued from the unbelievers in Judea, and that my service in Jerusalem will be welcomed by the saints there, [32] so that by God's will I can come to you with joy and find rest among you. [33] May the God of peace be with all of you. Amen.

[1] Let me introduce you to Phoebe, our sister, who serves the church at Cenchreae. [2] Welcome her in the Lord the way God's people should — and give her whatever she needs from you. She has been a patron to many, including me.

[3] Greet Prisca and Aquila — my partners in the work of Christ Jesus. [4] They put their own necks on the line to save my life. I'm not the only one grateful — every Gentile church owes them. [5] Greet the church that meets in their house too.

Greet my dear friend Epaenetus — the first convert in Asia for Christ.

[6] Greet Mary, who has worn herself out for you.

[7] Greet Andronicus and Junia, my kinsmen, my fellow prisoners — standouts among the apostles, and in Christ before I was.

[8] Greet Ampliatus, beloved to me in the Lord.

[9] Greet Urbanus, our co-worker in Christ, and my dear Stachys.

[10] Greet Apelles, tested and proven in Christ.

Greet the household of Aristobulus.

[11] Greet my kinsman Herodion. Greet those in the household of Narcissus who belong to the Lord.

[12] Greet Tryphaena and Tryphosa — women who labor in the Lord. Greet beloved Persis, who has labored hard in the Lord.

[13] Greet Rufus, chosen in the Lord, and his mother — who has been a mother to me as well.

[14] Greet Asyncritus, Phlegon, Hermes, Patrobas, Hermas, and the brothers and sisters with them.

[15] Greet Philologus and Julia, Nereus and his sister, Olympas, and all the saints with them.

[16] Greet one another with a holy kiss. All the churches of Christ send their greetings.

[17] One more thing, brothers and sisters — and listen carefully. Keep your eyes open for the ones who create division and lay traps that contradict the teaching you received. Walk away from them. [18] People like this aren't serving Christ our Lord — they're serving their own appetites. With smooth talk and pious flattery they reel in the unsuspecting. [19] Your obedience is famous everywhere — that thrills me — but I want you wise about what is good and untouched by what is evil. [20] The God of peace will crush Satan under your feet, and soon.

The grace of our Lord Jesus be with you.

[21] Timothy, my co-worker, sends greetings, along with Lucius, Jason, and Sosipater — my kinsmen.

[22] I, Tertius, who wrote this letter down, greet you in the Lord.

[23] Gaius — host to me and to the whole church — sends greetings. So does Erastus, the city treasurer, and Quartus, our brother.

[25] To the One who has the power to make you stand firm — according to my gospel, according to the proclamation of Jesus Christ, according to the unveiling of the mystery kept under wraps through long ages of silence [26] but now thrown open, made known through the prophetic writings by command of the eternal God, to bring all the nations to the obedience of faith — [27] to the only wise God, through Jesus Christ, be the glory forever. Amen.

[1] Back in the days when the judges ruled, a famine struck the land. A man from Bethlehem in Judah packed up his wife and two sons and went to live as a foreigner in the country of Moab.

[2] His name was Elimelech, his wife was Naomi, and their sons were Mahlon and Kilion — Ephrathites from Bethlehem in Judah. They reached Moab and settled in.

[3] Then Elimelech, Naomi's husband, died. She was left with her two sons.

[4] The sons married Moabite women — one named Orpah, the other Ruth. They lived there about ten years.

[5] Then both Mahlon and Kilion died too. Naomi was left — no husband, no sons. Nothing.

[6] Word reached her in Moab that the Lord had finally visited his people and given them bread. So she got up to go home, her daughters-in-law with her.

[7] She walked out of the place where she had buried her life, the two young women at her side, and they started down the road back to Judah.

[8] Then Naomi turned to them. "Go. Both of you. Go back to your mothers' homes. May the Lord show you the same loyal love you showed our dead — and me.

[9] May he grant each of you rest in a new husband's house." She kissed them. They wept, loud and broken.

[10] "No," they said. "We're going with you. Back to your people."

[11] "Turn around, my daughters," Naomi said. "Why come with me? Do I have more sons inside me who could grow up and be your husbands?

[12] Turn back, my daughters. Go. I'm too old for another man. Even if I said tonight there's still hope — even if I had a husband tonight and bore sons —

[13] would you wait for them to grow up? Would you stay unmarried for them? No, my daughters. It's worse for me than for you. The Lord's hand has come down against me."

[14] They wept again. Then Orpah kissed her mother-in-law goodbye. But Ruth clung to her.

[15] "Look," Naomi said, "your sister-in-law is going home to her people and her gods. Go with her."

[16] But Ruth said: "Stop pushing me away. Stop telling me to leave you. Wherever you go, I go. Wherever you stay, I stay. Your people, my people. Your God, my God.

[17] Where you die, I'll die — and there I'll be buried. May the Lord do terrible things to me if anything but death separates us."

[18] When Naomi saw she was set on it — fully, immovably — she stopped arguing.

[19] So the two of them walked on together until they came to Bethlehem. When they arrived, the whole town stirred. "Is this Naomi?" the women said.

[20] "Don't call me Naomi," she told them. "Call me Mara — bitter. The Almighty has made my life bitter.

[21] I went out full. The Lord brought me back empty. Why call me Naomi? The Lord has testified against me. The Almighty has broken me."

[22] So Naomi came back from Moab, and with her came Ruth the Moabite, her daughter-in-law. They arrived in Bethlehem just as the barley harvest was beginning.

[1] Naomi had a relative on her husband's side — a man of standing, from Elimelech's clan. His name was Boaz.

[2] Ruth the Moabite said to Naomi, "Let me go out to the fields and glean behind whoever shows me kindness." Naomi said, "Go ahead, daughter."

[3] So she went. She came to a field and gleaned behind the harvesters. As it turned out — though "turned out" is the wrong word for it — the field belonged to Boaz, from Elimelech's clan.

[4] Right then, Boaz arrived from Bethlehem. He called to his harvesters, "The LORD be with you." They called back, "The LORD bless you."

[5] Boaz said to the foreman over his harvesters, "That young woman — whose is she?"

[6] The foreman answered, "She's the Moabite. The one who came back with Naomi from the country of Moab.

[7] She asked if she could glean — gather among the sheaves behind the harvesters. She came, and she's been on her feet since morning. Hasn't rested but a moment in the shelter."

[8] Boaz went to Ruth. "Listen, daughter. Don't glean in any other field. Don't leave here. Stay close to my young women.

[9] Keep your eyes on the field they're harvesting, and follow them. I've ordered the men not to lay a hand on you. When you're thirsty, go to the jars and drink what the men have drawn."

[10] She dropped face-down to the ground. "Why this? Why have I found favor in your eyes — that you would notice me, a foreigner?"

[11] Boaz answered, "I've heard everything. Everything you did for your mother-in-law after your husband died. How you left your father and mother and your homeland and came to a people you'd never known.

[12] May the LORD repay what you've done. May your wages be full from the LORD, the God of Israel — under whose wings you've come to take shelter."

[13] She said, "May I keep finding favor in your eyes, my lord. You've comforted me. You've spoken to the heart of your servant — though I'm not even one of your servant girls."

[14] At mealtime Boaz said to her, "Come over. Eat some bread. Dip your piece in the wine vinegar." She sat beside the harvesters. He passed her roasted grain. She ate her fill, and there was some left over.

[15] When she got up to glean again, Boaz gave his men orders: "Let her glean even among the sheaves. Don't shame her.

[16] In fact — pull some stalks out of the bundles on purpose. Leave them for her. Don't rebuke her."

[17] So she gleaned in the field until evening. Then she beat out what she'd gathered — about thirty pounds of barley.

[18] She hoisted it and went back to town. Her mother-in-law saw what she'd gleaned. Ruth also pulled out what she'd saved from her meal and gave it to her.

[19] Naomi said, "Where did you glean today? Where did you work? Blessed be the man who took notice of you." So Ruth told her mother-in-law whose field she'd worked in. "The man's name," she said, "is Boaz."

[20] Naomi said to her daughter-in-law, "Blessed be he by the LORD, who has not abandoned his kindness — not to the living, not to the dead." Then Naomi added, "The man is a close relative. One of our redeemers."

[21] Ruth the Moabite said, "He also told me, 'Stay close to my workers until they've finished all my harvest.'"

[22] Naomi said, "Good, daughter. Go out with his young women. Better that than being harassed in some other field."

[23] So Ruth stayed close to Boaz's young women, gleaning until the barley harvest and the wheat harvest were both done. And she lived with her mother-in-law.

[1] One day Naomi said to her, "My daughter, shouldn't I be looking for a place where you can finally settle down — somewhere good for you?

[2] Listen — Boaz, our kinsman, the one whose women you've been working alongside, he's winnowing barley at the threshing floor tonight.

[3] So: wash up. Put on perfume. Dress in your best. Then go down to the threshing floor — but don't let him see you until he's finished eating and drinking.

[4] When he lies down, mark the spot. Then go over, uncover his feet, and lie down there. He'll tell you what to do next."

[5] Ruth said, "Everything you've told me — I'll do."

[6] So she went down to the threshing floor and did exactly what her mother-in-law had told her.

[7] Boaz ate, drank, and his heart was light. He went off to lie down at the edge of the grain pile. She came in quietly, uncovered his feet, and lay down.

[8] In the middle of the night something startled him. He turned over — and there was a woman lying at his feet.

[9] "Who are you?" he said. "I am Ruth, your servant," she answered. "Spread the corner of your cloak over me — you are a redeemer."

[10] "May the LORD bless you, my daughter," he said. "This loyalty you're showing now is greater than what came before — you haven't chased after younger men, rich or poor.

[11] So don't be afraid. Whatever you ask, I'll do. Everyone in town knows what kind of woman you are — a woman of strength.

[12] Yes, it's true I'm a redeemer. But there's another man closer in line than I am.

[13] Stay here tonight. In the morning, if he wants to redeem you — fine, let him. But if he won't, then as the LORD lives, I will. Lie down until morning."

[14] So she lay at his feet until dawn, then rose before anyone could recognize a face. He said, "Don't let it be known a woman came to the threshing floor."

[15] Then he said, "Bring me the shawl you're wearing — hold it open." She did, and he measured out six portions of barley into it, lifted it onto her, and headed back to town.

[16] When she came to her mother-in-law, Naomi asked, "How did it go, my daughter?" And Ruth told her everything the man had done for her.

[17] "He gave me these six measures of barley," she said. "He told me, 'Don't go back to your mother-in-law empty-handed.'"

[18] Naomi said, "Sit tight, my daughter, until you see how this falls out. That man won't rest until he's settled it — today."

[1] Boaz went up to the city gate and sat down there. And the very kinsman-redeemer he'd mentioned walked by. Boaz called out, "Friend, come over here. Sit." The man turned aside and sat down.

[2] Then Boaz gathered ten of the city elders and said, "Sit with us." They sat.

[3] Boaz turned to the redeemer. "Naomi — back from Moab — is selling the parcel of land that belonged to our relative Elimelech.

[4] I figured you should know. So here it is: buy it, in front of these men sitting here, in front of the elders of my people. If you'll redeem it, redeem it. If not, tell me straight, because I'm next in line after you — there's no one else." The man said, "I'll redeem it."

[5] Then Boaz said, "The day you buy the field from Naomi's hand, you also acquire Ruth the Moabite, the widow of the dead man — to raise up the dead man's name on his inheritance."

[6] The redeemer said, "Then I can't redeem it. I'd ruin my own inheritance. You take it. Take my right of redemption. I can't."

[7] (Now this was the old custom in Israel for sealing any matter of redemption or exchange: a man would pull off his sandal and hand it to the other. That was how Israel notarized a deal.)

[8] So the redeemer said to Boaz, "Buy it yourself." And he pulled off his sandal.

[9] Boaz turned to the elders and all the people. "You are witnesses today. I have acquired from Naomi's hand everything that belonged to Elimelech, and everything that belonged to Chilion and Mahlon.

[10] And Ruth the Moabite, Mahlon's widow — I have acquired her as my wife, to raise up the dead man's name on his inheritance, so his name won't be cut off from his brothers or from the gate of his hometown. You are witnesses today."

[11] All the people at the gate, and the elders, said, "We are witnesses. May the LORD make this woman coming into your house like Rachel and Leah — the two who built the house of Israel. May you prosper in Ephrathah and be renowned in Bethlehem.

[12] May your house become like the house of Perez, whom Tamar bore to Judah, through the offspring the LORD gives you by this young woman."

[13] So Boaz took Ruth, and she became his wife. He went to her, and the LORD gave her conception, and she bore a son.

[14] The women said to Naomi, "Blessed be the LORD, who did not leave you today without a redeemer. May his name ring out in Israel!

[15] He will restore your life and sustain you in your old age — because your daughter-in-law who loves you, who is worth more to you than seven sons, has given birth to him."

[16] Naomi took the child, laid him on her lap, and became his nurse.

[17] The neighborhood women named him. "A son has been born to Naomi!" they said. They called him Obed. He became the father of Jesse, the father of David.

[18] These are the generations of Perez: Perez fathered Hezron,

[19] Hezron fathered Ram, Ram fathered Amminadab,

[20] Amminadab fathered Nahshon, Nahshon fathered Salmon,

[21] Salmon fathered Boaz, Boaz fathered Obed,

[22] Obed fathered Jesse, and Jesse fathered David.

[1] The Song of Songs, Solomon's masterpiece.

[2] Kiss me — kiss me with the kisses of your mouth.
Your love is sweeter than wine.
[3] The scent of you intoxicates;
your name itself is perfume poured out —
no wonder the young women are in love with you.
[4] Take me with you. Let's run.
The king has led me into his chambers.

We will celebrate you, we will rejoice in you.
We will praise your love above wine —
and rightly they adore you.

[5] I am dark, and I am beautiful,
daughters of Jerusalem —
dark like the tents of Kedar,
beautiful like Solomon's curtains.
[6] Don't stare at me because I'm dark,
because the sun has gazed upon me.
My mother's sons were furious with me;
they put me to work guarding the vineyards —
but my own vineyard I could not keep.

[7] Tell me, you whom my soul loves —
where do you graze your flock?
Where do you let them rest at noon?
Why should I wander like a veiled stranger
among the flocks of your companions?

[8] If you don't know, most beautiful of women,
follow the tracks of the flock,
and pasture your young goats
beside the shepherds' tents.

[9] My love, I compare you
to a mare among Pharaoh's chariots.
[10] Your cheeks are lovely framed with jewels,
your neck strung with strands of beads.
[11] We will make you ornaments of gold,
studded with silver.

[12] While the king reclined at his table,
my nard released its fragrance.
[13] My beloved is a sachet of myrrh to me,
resting all night between my breasts.
[14] My beloved is a cluster of henna blossoms
in the vineyards of En-gedi.

[15] You are beautiful, my love —
you are beautiful.
Your eyes are doves.

[16] You are beautiful, my beloved —
you are radiant.
And our bed is a canopy of green.
[17] The beams of our house are cedar,
our rafters cypress.

[1] I am the wild rose of Sharon, the lily blooming in the valley.

[2] Like a lily among thorns — that is my love among the daughters of men.

[3] Like an apple tree standing in a forest of scrub — that is my beloved among the sons of men. I sat down in his shade, dizzy with delight, and his fruit was sweet on my tongue.

[4] He brought me to the banquet hall, and the banner he raised over me was love.

[5] Hold me up with raisin cakes, revive me with apples — I am sick with love.

[6] His left hand cradles my head, his right hand pulls me close.

[7] Daughters of Jerusalem, I charge you by the gazelles and the wild does of the field: do not stir up love, do not awaken it, until it pleases.

[8] Listen — my beloved! Here he comes, leaping over the mountains, vaulting the hills.

[9] My beloved is a gazelle, a young stag. There — he is already at our wall, peering through the windows, glancing through the lattice.

[10] My beloved spoke, and this is what he said to me: Rise up, my love, my beautiful one — come away with me.

[11] Look — the winter is finished, the rains are gone, vanished.

[12] Flowers are erupting from the ground, the season of singing has arrived, the voice of the dove echoes across our land.

[13] The fig tree is ripening its early fruit, the vines are in bloom, breathing out their fragrance. Rise up, my love, my beautiful one — come away with me.

[14] My dove, hidden in the cleft of the rock, tucked into the secret places of the cliff — let me see your face, let me hear your voice. Your voice is sweet, your face is lovely.

[15] Catch the foxes for us — the little foxes ruining the vineyards, our vineyards heavy with blossom.

[16] My beloved is mine, and I am his — he grazes among the lilies.

[17] Until the day breathes its first light and the shadows flee, turn to me, my beloved — be like a gazelle, like a young stag on the rugged hills.

[1] All night long on my bed I reached for him — the one my soul aches for. I reached, and the place beside me was empty.

[2] So I rose. I have to find him. I moved through the city — every street, every square — searching for the one my soul aches for. I searched. I did not find him.

[3] The watchmen found me, making their rounds through the city. "The one my soul loves — have you seen him?"

[4] Barely past them, and there he was. I seized him. I would not let go — not until I'd brought him into my mother's house, into the very room where I was conceived.

[5] Daughters of Jerusalem, I charge you by the gazelles, by the wild does of the field: do not stir love, do not wake it, until it is ready.

[6] What is this, rising from the wilderness like a column of smoke — perfumed with myrrh and frankincense, with every spice the merchant carries?

[7] Look — Solomon's carriage. Sixty warriors surround it, the elite of Israel,

[8] every man with a sword on his thigh, trained for battle, ready for the terrors of the night.

[9] King Solomon built this carriage for himself from the cedars of Lebanon.

[10] He set its posts in silver, its base in gold, its seat in royal purple — and the interior, lovingly inlaid by the daughters of Jerusalem.

[11] Come out, daughters of Zion. Look at King Solomon — wearing the crown his mother set on his head the day of his wedding, the day his heart overflowed.

[1] You are stunning, my love—stunning. Your eyes behind your veil are doves. Your hair spills like a flock of goats streaming down the slopes of Gilead. [2] Your teeth—a flock of sheep just shorn, climbing up from the washing, each one matched with its twin, not one of them missing. [3] Your lips, a scarlet thread; your mouth, lovely. Behind your veil, your temples glow like a slice of pomegranate. [4] Your neck rises like the tower of David, built in courses of stone, hung with a thousand shields, every one of them belonging to warriors. [5] Your breasts—two fawns, twin gazelles grazing among the lilies. [6] Until the day breathes and the shadows flee, I am going to the mountain of myrrh, to the hill of frankincense. [7] Every inch of you is beautiful, my love. There is no flaw in you. [8] Come away with me from Lebanon, my bride—come away from Lebanon. Descend from the peak of Amana, from the summit of Senir and Hermon, from the lions' dens, from the mountain haunts of leopards. [9] You have stolen my heart, my sister, my bride. You have stolen my heart with a single glance of your eyes, with one jewel of your necklace. [10] How beautiful is your love, my sister, my bride. Your love is better than wine; the scent of your perfumes outshines every spice. [11] Your lips drip honey, bride of mine; honey and milk are under your tongue, and the fragrance of your garments is the fragrance of Lebanon itself. [12] A locked garden is my sister, my bride—a sealed spring, a fountain shut up tight. [13] Your branches are an orchard of pomegranates with the choicest fruit, henna and nard, [14] nard and saffron, calamus and cinnamon, every kind of frankincense tree, myrrh and aloes—every finest spice. [15] You are a garden fountain, a well of living water, streams flowing down from Lebanon. [16] Wake up, north wind. Come, south wind. Breathe across my garden, let its fragrance pour out. Let my beloved come into his garden and taste its choicest fruit.

[1] I've come into my garden, my sister, my bride. I've gathered my myrrh with my spice, eaten my honeycomb with my honey, drunk my wine with my milk.

Friends — eat. Lovers — drink. Drink deep.

[2] I was sleeping, but my heart stayed awake. Listen — my lover is knocking.

"Open to me, my sister, my darling, my dove, my flawless one. My head is drenched with dew, my hair soaked with the night."

[3] But I had stripped off my robe — must I put it back on? I had washed my feet — must I dirty them again?

[4] He slid his hand through the latch-opening, and everything in me lurched toward him.

[5] I rose to open for my lover, my hands dripping myrrh, my fingers running with myrrh, soaking the bolt of the lock.

[6] I opened for my lover — but my lover had turned, had gone. Something in me died when he spoke and I missed him. I searched, couldn't find him. I called, no answer.

[7] The watchmen patrolling the city found me. They beat me. They wounded me. The guards on the walls — they tore off my shawl.

[8] Swear to me, daughters of Jerusalem — if you find my lover, what will you tell him? Tell him I am sick with love.

[9] What makes your lover better than any other, you most beautiful of women? What sets your lover apart, that you put us under oath?

[10] My lover — radiant and ruddy, one in ten thousand.

[11] His head, finest gold. His hair, wavy, raven-black.

[12] His eyes — doves beside streams of water, washed in milk, set like jewels.

[13] His cheeks — beds of spice yielding perfume. His lips — lilies dripping liquid myrrh.

[14] His arms — rods of gold studded with topaz. His body — polished ivory inlaid with sapphires.

[15] His legs — pillars of marble set on bases of pure gold. His presence — like Lebanon, towering, like its cedars.

[16] His mouth — sweetness itself. Everything about him — desire. This is my lover. This is my friend, daughters of Jerusalem.

[1] Where did your beloved go, you most beautiful of women? Which way did he turn? Tell us — we'll search with you.

[2] My beloved went down to his garden, to the beds of spices, to graze among the gardens and gather lilies.

[3] I am his and he is mine — the one who feeds among the lilies.

[4] You are beautiful, my love, like Tirzah — lovely as Jerusalem, terrible as an army with banners raised.

[5] Turn your eyes from me — they overwhelm me. Your hair flows like a flock pouring down from Gilead.

[6] Your teeth — a flock of ewes climbing up from the washing, each one matched, none of them missing.

[7] Behind your veil, your temple glows like a slice of pomegranate.

[8] Sixty queens, eighty concubines, more young women than anyone can count —

[9] but my dove, my flawless one, stands alone. Her mother's only daughter, the radiant favorite of the one who bore her. The young women see her and call her blessed; queens and concubines praise her.

[10] Who is this rising like the dawn — beautiful as the moon, brilliant as the sun, terrible as an army with banners raised?

[11] I went down to the walnut grove to see the new growth in the valley, to see if the vine had budded, if the pomegranates were in bloom.

[12] Before I knew it, my own desire had set me among the chariots of my noble people.

[13] Come back, come back, Shulammite — come back, come back, let us look at you. Why do you stare at the Shulammite as if she were the dance of two armies?

[1] Your feet in those sandals — what beauty, daughter of nobility. The curves of your thighs are jewels, the work of a master craftsman. [2] Your navel a rounded chalice, never lacking its blended wine. Your waist a mound of wheat fenced in by lilies. [3] Your breasts — twin fawns, a gazelle's matched pair. [4] Your neck rises like an ivory tower. Your eyes — the deep pools of Heshbon at the gate of Bath-rabbim. Your nose, the tower of Lebanon, keeping watch toward Damascus. [5] Your head crowns you like Carmel, your hair like royal purple — a king sits captured in its strands.

[6] How beautiful, how delightful you are, love — every pleasure caught up in you. [7] Your stature is the palm tree's stature; your breasts, its clusters. [8] So I said: I'm climbing that palm. I'm taking hold of the fruit. Let your breasts be clusters of the vine, the scent of your breath like apples, [9] your mouth the finest wine —

— flowing smooth to my beloved, sliding past sleeping lips.

[10] I belong to him. And his longing reaches for me.

[11] Come, my love — let's leave for the open country, spend the night among the henna blossoms. [12] At first light we'll go to the vineyards, see if the vines have budded, if the blossoms have opened, if the pomegranates are in bloom. There I'll give you my love. [13] The mandrakes are pouring out their fragrance, and over our doorway every delicacy waits — new and old, all of them — I've stored them up for you, my beloved.

[1] If only you were my brother, nursed at my mother's breast — then I could find you in the street and kiss you, and no one would despise me for it.

[2] I would lead you, bring you into my mother's house — she would teach me. I would give you spiced wine to drink, the fresh juice of my pomegranate.

[3] His left hand cradles my head, his right hand holds me close.

[4] Daughters of Jerusalem — I charge you: do not wake love, do not stir it, until it wants to rise.

[5] Who is this rising from the wilderness, leaning on the one she loves? Under the apple tree I woke you — there, where your mother labored to bring you forth, where she who bore you cried out and you began.

[6] Set me as a seal over your heart, as a seal on your arm — because love is fierce as death, jealousy unyielding as the grave. Its sparks are sparks of fire, the very flame of the LORD.

[7] Floods cannot quench love, rivers cannot drown it. If a man offered every penny of his fortune for love, he would be laughed out of the room.

[8] We have a little sister, and her breasts have not yet come in. What shall we do for our sister on the day she is spoken for?

[9] If she is a wall, we will build silver towers on her. If she is a door, we will brace her with cedar planks.

[10] I am a wall — and my breasts are towers. So in his eyes I became one who brings peace.

[11] Solomon had a vineyard at Baal Hamon. He let it out to keepers; each one was to bring in a thousand silver pieces for the fruit.

[12] My vineyard, my own, is mine alone. Keep your thousand, Solomon — and two hundred to those who tend the fruit. I keep what is mine.

[13] You who dwell in the gardens — companions are listening for your voice. Let me hear it.

[14] Run to me, my love — be like a gazelle, like a young stag bounding over the mountains of spices.

[1] Paul, slave of God and emissary of Jesus the Anointed, sent to build up the faith of God's chosen and lead them into the full knowledge of truth — the kind of truth that produces a godly life,

[2] anchored in the hope of life that never ends. This is the life God — who does not lie — promised before time had a name,

[3] and at the right moment he made his word public through the proclamation entrusted to me by command of God our Savior.

[4] To Titus, my true son in the faith we share: grace and peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Savior.

[5] I left you in Crete for a reason: finish what I started. Put the loose ends in order. Appoint elders in every town, exactly as I instructed you.

[6] Here is the bar: a man beyond reproach. Faithful to one wife. His children believers — not running wild, not insubordinate.

[7] An overseer manages God's household, so he has to be blameless. Not arrogant. Not hot-tempered. Not a drunk. Not violent. Not greedy for a quick profit.

[8] Instead: hospitable, in love with what is good, level-headed, just, devout, disciplined.

[9] He must hold tight to the trustworthy message he was taught, so he can both encourage people with healthy teaching and shut down those who twist it.

[10] Because there are plenty of those — rebels, empty talkers, con men, especially from the circumcision crowd.

[11] They have to be silenced. They are wrecking entire households, peddling lies for cash.

[12] One of their own prophets put it like this: "Cretans — always liars, vicious animals, lazy gluttons."

[13] And he was right. So confront them sharply — make their faith healthy again,

[14] so they stop chasing Jewish myths and the rules invented by people who have turned their backs on the truth.

[15] To the pure, everything is pure. But to the corrupt and faithless, nothing is pure — their minds are poisoned, their consciences ruined.

[16] They claim to know God, but their lives scream the opposite. They are revolting, defiant, useless for anything good.

[1] You, though — teach what fits with the truth that holds weight.

[2] Older men: sober, dignified, level-headed, sound in faith, in love, in endurance.

[3] Older women, the same — let their bearing match the holy ground they stand on. No tongues sharpened for gossip, no slavery to wine. Teachers of what is good.

[4] Let them school the younger women — to love their husbands, love their children,

[5] to keep their heads, stay pure, manage their homes well, lean toward kindness, defer to their husbands — so no one finds room to slander the word of God.

[6] The younger men, likewise — urge them to keep themselves in hand.

[7] In everything, make yourself the pattern. Your teaching: clean, weighty, beyond reproach.

[8] Your words: solid, unanswerable — so that anyone setting himself against you walks away embarrassed, with nothing to throw.

[9] Slaves: submit to your masters in every situation. Aim to please. Don't talk back.

[10] Don't pilfer. Show yourselves entirely trustworthy — so that in everything, you make the teaching about God our Savior beautiful.

[11] Because the grace of God has broken through — salvation for everyone, in plain sight.

[12] And that same grace is training us: turn from godlessness, turn from the appetites of this age, and live — right now, in this present world — with restraint, with integrity, with God at the center.

[13] We are waiting for the blessed hope — the appearing in glory of our great God and Savior, Jesus the Messiah.

[14] He gave himself for us. To buy us out of every lawless thing. To scrub us clean. To make us his — a people of his own, hungry for what is good.

[15] These are the things you speak. Urge them. Confront with them. With every ounce of authority you carry. Let no one dismiss you.

[1] Drill this into them: submit to rulers and authorities, obey the government, stand ready for whatever good work comes their way. [2] Speak ill of no one. Pick no fights. Be reasonable. Show every person genuine gentleness.

[3] Remember — we were once the fools. Disobedient. Lost. Enslaved to every craving and pleasure we could chase. Living in malice and envy. Hated and hating each other. [4] Then the kindness of God our Savior broke through. His love for humanity appeared. [5] And he saved us — not because of righteous things we'd pulled off, but because of his mercy. He saved us through the washing of rebirth and the renewing work of the Holy Spirit, [6] which he poured out on us lavishly through Jesus the Messiah our Savior, [7] so that, justified by his grace, we'd become heirs — with the hope of life that never ends.

[8] This is rock solid. And I want you to press this point relentlessly, so that those who've trusted God will throw themselves into doing good. These things matter. These things actually help people.

[9] But the foolish debates, the genealogies, the squabbles, the fights about the law — sidestep them. They're useless. They go nowhere. [10] Someone stirring up division? Warn him once. Warn him twice. Then be done with him. [11] You know what kind of person he is — twisted, self-condemned, sinning with his eyes wide open.

[12] When I send Artemas or Tychicus to you, drop everything and come to me at Nicopolis — I've decided to winter there. [13] Send Zenas the lawyer and Apollos off well-supplied. Make sure they lack nothing for the journey. [14] And our people need to learn this too: devote themselves to good work, meet pressing needs, refuse to live unfruitful lives.

[15] Everyone with me sends greetings. Greet those who love us in the faith. Grace be with all of you.

[1] In the eighth month of Darius's second year, the word of the LORD came to Zechariah son of Berechiah, son of Iddo, the prophet:

[2] The LORD was furious with your fathers. Furious.

[3] So tell them — this is what the LORD of Armies says: Come back to me, and I will come back to you. The LORD of Armies has spoken.

[4] Don't be like your fathers. The prophets of old shouted at them: Turn from your wicked ways, your wicked deeds — this is what the LORD of Armies says. But they wouldn't listen. They wouldn't pay me any attention. The LORD has spoken.

[5] Where are your fathers now? And the prophets — do they live forever?

[6] But my words, my decrees — the ones I commanded my servants the prophets — those caught up with your fathers, didn't they? And they came around. They said, "The LORD of Armies has done to us exactly what he said he would, exactly what our ways and deeds deserved."

[7] On the twenty-fourth day of the eleventh month — the month of Shebat — in Darius's second year, the word of the LORD came to Zechariah son of Berechiah, son of Iddo, the prophet. This is what he saw:

[8] In the dead of night, I saw him — a man riding a red horse, standing among the myrtle trees in the ravine. Behind him: horses — red, sorrel, white.

[9] "What are these, my lord?" I asked. The angel speaking with me said, "I'll show you what they are."

[10] Then the man standing among the myrtles spoke up: "These are the ones the LORD sent to patrol the earth."

[11] And they reported to the angel of the LORD standing among the myrtles: "We've patrolled the earth. Everything is quiet. Everything is still."

[12] Then the angel of the LORD said, "LORD of Armies — how long? How long until you show mercy to Jerusalem and the cities of Judah? You've been angry with them for seventy years now."

[13] And the LORD answered the angel speaking with me — answered with words that were good, words that were comfort.

[14] Then the angel told me: Proclaim this. The LORD of Armies says: I burn for Jerusalem. I burn for Zion. The jealousy in me is fierce.

[15] And I am furious — absolutely furious — with the nations that sit so comfortably. I was only a little angry, but they piled on the disaster.

[16] So here is what the LORD says: I am returning to Jerusalem with mercy. My house will be rebuilt there — the LORD of Armies declares it — and the measuring line will be stretched across Jerusalem again.

[17] Proclaim this too. The LORD of Armies says: My cities will once again brim over with good. The LORD will comfort Zion once more. He will choose Jerusalem — again.

[18] Then I looked up — and there they were: four horns.

[19] "What are these?" I asked the angel speaking with me. He said, "These are the horns that scattered Judah, Israel, and Jerusalem."

[20] Then the LORD showed me four craftsmen.

[21] "What are they coming to do?" I asked. He answered: "Those horns scattered Judah — scattered them so completely no one could lift his head. But these have come to terrify the horns, to hack down the horns of the nations that raised them against the land of Judah to scatter her."

[1] I looked up and saw a man with a measuring line in his hand.

[2] "Where are you going?" I asked.

He said, "To measure Jerusalem — to see how wide it stretches, how far it runs."

[3] Then the messenger who had been speaking with me stepped forward, and another messenger came out to meet him,

[4] saying, "Run. Tell that young man this: Jerusalem will sprawl beyond its walls — a city without limits, overflowing with people and livestock.

[5] And I myself — declares the LORD — will be a wall of fire around her, and the glory blazing in her midst."

[6] Up! Up! Flee from the land of the north — declares the LORD — for I scattered you to the four winds of heaven. So says the LORD.

[7] Up, Zion! Escape — you who still live in Daughter Babylon!

[8] For this is what the LORD of Armies says — and his glory sent me — to the nations that plundered you: whoever touches you touches the pupil of his eye.

[9] Watch me — I will swing my hand against them, and they will become spoil for those they once enslaved. Then you will know the LORD of Armies sent me.

[10] Sing, Daughter Zion! Shout for joy! I am coming, and I will live in your midst — declares the LORD.

[11] On that day, nation after nation will bind themselves to the LORD and become his people. He will dwell in your midst, and you will know the LORD of Armies sent me to you.

[12] The LORD will take Judah as his portion in the holy land, and Jerusalem — once more — will be his chosen.

[13] Silence, all flesh, before the LORD — he has roused himself from his holy dwelling.

[1] Then he showed me Joshua, the high priest, standing before the Angel of the LORD — and the Accuser stood at his right hand, ready to bring charges.

[2] And the LORD said to the Accuser: "The LORD silences you, Satan. The LORD who has chosen Jerusalem silences you. Is this man not a stick pulled from the fire?"

[3] Joshua stood there before the Angel, dressed in clothes caked with filth.

[4] The Angel spoke to those standing before him: "Strip the filthy clothes off him." Then he turned to Joshua and said, "Look — I have lifted your guilt off you. I am clothing you in robes of glory."

[5] And I said, "Put a clean turban on his head." So they crowned him with a clean turban and dressed him, while the Angel of the LORD stood watching.

[6] Then the Angel of the LORD gave Joshua this charge:

[7] "This is what the LORD of Hosts says: If you walk in my ways and keep my watch, then you will govern my house, you will guard my courts, and I will give you free passage among these who stand here.

[8] Listen, Joshua, high priest — you and your fellow priests seated before you, men who are a sign of what is coming: I am bringing my servant, the Branch.

[9] Look at the stone I have set before Joshua — a single stone with seven eyes. I will engrave its inscription myself, declares the LORD of Hosts, and I will wipe away the guilt of this land in one day.

[10] On that day — this is the word of the LORD of Hosts — every one of you will invite his neighbor under the shade of his vine and his fig tree."

[1] The messenger who had been speaking with me came back and woke me — like a man roused from sleep.

[2] "What do you see?" he asked.

I said, "I see a lampstand — all gold. A bowl crowning the top of it, seven lamps mounted on it, seven channels feeding each lamp.

[3] And two olive trees flanking it — one on the right of the bowl, one on the left."

[4] I turned to the messenger. "What are these, my lord?"

[5] He said, "You don't know?"

I said, "No, my lord."

[6] Then he said: "This is the word of the LORD to Zerubbabel — Not by force. Not by power. By my Spirit, says the LORD of Armies.

[7] Who do you think you are, mountain in his way? Before Zerubbabel you'll level out into a plain. He'll bring out the capstone with shouts of 'Grace! Grace to it!'"

[8] The word of the LORD came to me again:

[9] "Zerubbabel's hands laid this foundation. Zerubbabel's hands will finish it. Then you'll know the LORD of Armies sent me to you.

[10] Who dared despise the day of small beginnings? They'll celebrate when they see the plumb line in Zerubbabel's hand — those seven, the eyes of the LORD that range across the whole earth."

[11] Then I asked him, "What are these two olive trees, on the right and left of the lampstand?"

[12] And again: "What are these two olive branches, pouring golden oil through the two gold pipes?"

[13] He said, "You don't know what these are?"

I said, "No, my lord."

[14] He said, "These are the two anointed ones — those who stand beside the Lord of all the earth."

[1] I looked up again, and there it was — a scroll, airborne, unrolling as it flew.

[2] The messenger asked, "What do you see?"

I said, "A flying scroll. Thirty feet long, fifteen feet wide."

[3] He said: "This is the curse going out across the whole land. According to one side, every thief gets purged. According to the other, everyone who swears falsely gets purged.

[4] I'm releasing it," declares the LORD of Armies. "It will enter the thief's house. It will enter the house of anyone who swears falsely by my name. It will settle in, lodge in the timbers and the stones, and consume the house entirely."

[5] Then the messenger who had been speaking with me stepped forward. "Look up," he said. "See what's coming out."

[6] "What is it?" I asked.

"A basket," he said. "A measuring basket, going out. This is what they look like — all of them — across the land."

[7] Then the lead lid lifted, and there was a woman, sitting inside the basket.

[8] "This," he said, "is Wickedness." He shoved her back into the basket and slammed the lead weight down on the opening.

[9] I looked up, and there they were — two women, gliding toward me, wind filling their wings. Their wings were like a stork's. They lifted the basket between earth and sky.

[10] "Where are they taking it?" I asked the messenger.

[11] "To the land of Shinar," he said, "to build it a house. When it's ready, the basket will be set down there, on its pedestal."

[1] I looked up again, and there they were—four chariots breaking out from between two mountains, and the mountains were bronze.

[2] The first chariot had red horses, the second had black,

[3] the third had white, the fourth had dappled—every one of them powerful.

[4] I said to the messenger speaking with me, "What are these, my lord?"

[5] He answered, "These are the four winds of heaven, going out from standing before the Lord of all the earth.

[6] The chariot with the black horses heads north. The white follow behind them. The dappled head south."

[7] The powerful ones came out straining to patrol the earth, and he said, "Go—patrol the earth." And they did.

[8] Then he cried out to me, "Look—the ones heading north have set my Spirit at rest in the northern land."

[9] The word of the Lord came to me:

[10] Take an offering from the exiles—from Heldai, Tobijah, and Jedaiah, who have arrived from Babylon. Go that same day to the house of Josiah son of Zephaniah, where they are staying.

[11] Take silver and gold, fashion a crown, and set it on the head of Joshua son of Jehozadak, the high priest.

[12] Tell him: This is what the Lord of Heaven's Armies says—Look at this man. His name is Branch. From where he stands, he will branch out, and he will build the temple of the Lord.

[13] Yes—he is the one who will build the temple of the Lord. He will wear royal honor. He will sit and rule from his throne. And there will be a priest beside the throne, and between the two of them, perfect peace.

[14] The crown will stay in the temple of the Lord as a memorial to Heldai, Tobijah, Jedaiah, and Hen son of Zephaniah.

[15] People from far away will come and build the temple of the Lord, and you will know that the Lord of Heaven's Armies sent me to you. This will happen—if you truly listen to the voice of the Lord your God.

[1] In the fourth year of King Darius, the word of the LORD came to Zechariah on the fourth day of the ninth month, the month of Kislev.

[2] The people of Bethel had sent Sharezer and Regem-Melech with their men to soften the face of the LORD,

[3] and to put a question to the priests at the house of the LORD of Armies and to the prophets: "Should we keep weeping and fasting in the fifth month, the way we have done for so many years now?"

[4] Then the word of the LORD of Armies came to me:

[5] "Tell all the people of the land and the priests this: When you fasted and mourned in the fifth month and the seventh, year after year for seventy years — was that fast really for me? For me?

[6] And when you ate and when you drank — were you not feeding yourselves, pleasing yourselves?

[7] Are these not the very words the LORD cried out through the prophets of old, back when Jerusalem was full and at peace, with cities surrounding her, when the Negev and the foothills were settled?"

[8] And the word of the LORD came to Zechariah:

[9] "This is what the LORD of Armies says: Render true justice. Show mercy and compassion, each to his brother.

[10] Do not crush the widow or the orphan, the immigrant or the poor. Do not even let your hearts plot evil against one another.

[11] But they refused to listen. They turned a stubborn shoulder. They stopped their ears so they would not hear.

[12] They made their hearts like flint so the law would not reach them, nor the words the LORD of Armies sent by his Spirit through the prophets who came before. So a great fury came from the LORD of Armies.

[13] And just as I called and they would not listen — so when they called, I would not listen, says the LORD of Armies.

[14] I scattered them with a whirlwind among nations they had never known. The land they left behind lay desolate behind them — no one coming, no one going. A pleasant land made wasteland."

[1] The word of the LORD of Hosts came again:

[2] This is what the LORD of Hosts says: I burn for Zion with a fierce love — I burn for her with a jealous fire.

[3] This is what the LORD says: I am coming back to Zion. I will live in the middle of Jerusalem again. And Jerusalem will be called the Faithful City, and the mountain of the LORD of Hosts will be called the Holy Mountain.

[4] This is what the LORD of Hosts says: Old men and old women will sit again in the public squares of Jerusalem, each one leaning on a cane because they have lived so long.

[5] And the streets of the city will be packed with boys and girls playing there.

[6] This is what the LORD of Hosts says: If this seems impossible to the survivors of this people in those days, does it seem impossible to me? — declares the LORD of Hosts.

[7] This is what the LORD of Hosts says: Watch — I am rescuing my people from the country where the sun rises and from the country where it sets.

[8] I will bring them home. They will live in the heart of Jerusalem. They will be my people, and I will be their God — in faithfulness, in righteousness.

[9] This is what the LORD of Hosts says: Steady your hands — you who in these very days are hearing these words from the mouths of the prophets who stood here when the foundation of the house of the LORD of Hosts was laid, when the temple was about to rise.

[10] Because before that day, there was no wage for a worker, no rent for a beast. No one could come or go in safety because of the enemy. I had turned everyone loose against his neighbor.

[11] But I am not the same toward the remnant of this people now as I was in the days before — declares the LORD of Hosts.

[12] Now there will be a sowing of peace: the vine will give its fruit, the ground will give its yield, the heavens will give their dew, and I will hand all of it over to the survivors of this people as their inheritance.

[13] And just as you were a curse-word among the nations, house of Judah and house of Israel, so I will rescue you — and you will become a blessing. Don't be afraid. Steady your hands.

[14] This is what the LORD of Hosts says: Just as I was set on bringing disaster on you when your ancestors provoked me — and I did not relent, says the LORD of Hosts —

[15] so now I have turned and set my heart on doing good to Jerusalem and to the house of Judah. Don't be afraid.

[16] These are the things you must do: Speak truth — every man to his neighbor. Render verdicts in your gates that are true and that make for peace.

[17] Do not plot evil against each other in your hearts. Do not love a lying oath. All of these things I hate — declares the LORD.

[18] And the word of the LORD of Hosts came to me:

[19] This is what the LORD of Hosts says: The fast of the fourth month, the fast of the fifth, the fast of the seventh, and the fast of the tenth — all of them will become for the house of Judah seasons of joy, of gladness, of festivals worth celebrating. So love truth. Love peace.

[20] This is what the LORD of Hosts says: Peoples are still coming — whole populations from city after city.

[21] The people of one city will go to another and say, Come on — let us go right now to beg the favor of the LORD, to seek out the LORD of Hosts. I'm going myself.

[22] And many peoples and powerful nations will come to seek the LORD of Hosts in Jerusalem, to beg his favor.

[23] This is what the LORD of Hosts says: In those days, ten men from every language of every nation will grab hold of the hem of one Jewish man's robe and say: We are going with you — because we have heard that God is with you.

[1] A dispatch. The word of the LORD has landed on Hadrach, and Damascus is where it comes to rest — because the eyes of humanity, and every tribe of Israel, are fixed on the LORD.

[2] Hamath too, sharing that border. And Tyre. And Sidon — for all their cleverness.

[3] Tyre built herself a fortress, piled up silver like dust, hoarded gold like mud in the streets.

[4] Watch — the Lord is about to strip her bare. He will hurl her wealth into the sea and burn her to the ground.

[5] Ashkelon will see it and shake. Gaza will writhe in agony. Ekron will watch her hope shrivel and die. Gaza loses her king. Ashkelon empties out.

[6] A mongrel people will settle in Ashdod, and I will gut the arrogance of the Philistines.

[7] I will rip the blood from their mouths, the abominations from between their teeth. What remains will belong to our God — they will become like a clan in Judah, and Ekron will be folded in like the Jebusites were.

[8] I will camp around my house as a guard, so no marauding army comes through again — no oppressor will set foot there anymore. I have seen it now with my own eyes.

[9] Shout it, daughter of Zion! Sing out loud, daughter of Jerusalem! Look — your king is coming to you. He is the just one. He is the one who saves. Lowly. Riding a donkey — a colt, the foal of a donkey.

[10] I will scrap the chariots from Ephraim, the warhorses from Jerusalem. The battle bow gets snapped in two. He will speak peace to the nations. His rule will roll from sea to sea, from the River out to the ends of the earth.

[11] And as for you — because of the blood of my covenant with you, I am springing your prisoners loose from the dry pit.

[12] Come back to the stronghold, you prisoners of hope! Today I am telling you straight: I will give you back double.

[13] I have bent Judah like a bow. I have loaded Ephraim into it. I am rousing your sons, Zion, against your sons, Greece — wielding you like a warrior's sword.

[14] Then the LORD will appear above them. His arrow will flash like lightning. The Sovereign LORD will sound the trumpet and march out in the whirlwinds of the south.

[15] The LORD of Armies will be a shield over them. They will devour. They will trample the sling-stones underfoot. They will drink and roar like men flush with wine — full to the brim like the bowl at the altar, drenched like its corners.

[16] On that day the LORD their God will rescue them, his people — his flock — and they will sparkle on his land like jewels in a crown.

[17] How good it will be. How beautiful. Grain will make the young men flourish, and new wine the young women.

[1] Ask the LORD for rain in the season of spring rain. He is the one who makes the storm clouds, who pours down the showers, who hands every field its harvest.

[2] The household idols mumble nonsense. The diviners see lies. The dreamers spin smoke and offer empty comfort. That's why the people wander like sheep — beaten down, with no shepherd in sight.

[3] My anger blazes against the shepherds. I will deal with the lead goats. The LORD of Heaven's Armies has visited his flock — the house of Judah — and he will make them his warhorse, glorious in battle.

[4] Out of Judah will come the cornerstone. Out of Judah, the tent peg. Out of Judah, the battle bow. Out of Judah, every commander.

[5] Together they will be warriors trampling enemies into the mud of the streets. They will fight, because the LORD is with them. The cavalry of empires will be put to shame.

[6] I will fortify the house of Judah. I will rescue the house of Joseph. I will bring them home, because I have mercy on them — and it will be as if I never cast them off at all. I am the LORD their God. I will answer.

[7] Ephraim will become a warrior. Their hearts will swell as if drunk on wine. Their children will see it and celebrate. Their hearts will dance in the LORD.

[8] I will whistle for them and gather them in — because I have ransomed them — and they will multiply just like before.

[9] Though I scattered them among the nations, in those distant places they will remember me. They will raise their children and come back.

[10] I will bring them home from Egypt. I will gather them out of Assyria. I will pack them into Gilead and Lebanon until there's no room left to stand.

[11] He will cross through the sea of trouble and strike the waves down. The deeps of the Nile will run dry. Assyria's arrogance will be broken. Egypt's scepter will fall.

[12] I will make them strong in the LORD, and in his name they will walk. This is the declaration of the LORD.

[1] Throw open your doors, Lebanon — let the fire come for your cedars!

[2] Wail, you cypress, because the cedar has fallen, the mighty ones are wrecked. Wail, oaks of Bashan — the impenetrable forest is going down.

[3] Listen — the shepherds are howling. Their glory is in ruins. Listen — the young lions are roaring. The pride of the Jordan is laid waste.

[4] This is what the LORD my God says: Shepherd the flock marked for slaughter.

[5] Their buyers butcher them and walk away clean. Their sellers say, "Blessed be the LORD — I've turned a profit!" And their own shepherds feel nothing for them.

[6] So I will feel nothing for the people of this land, the LORD declares. Watch — I will hand every person over to their neighbor, into the grip of their king. They will smash the land to pieces, and I will not lift a finger to save anyone.

[7] So I shepherded the flock marked for slaughter — the most miserable of the sheep. I took two staffs. One I named Favor. The other I named Union. And I shepherded the flock.

[8] In a single month I cut down three shepherds. I was done with them, and they detested me.

[9] So I said: I will not shepherd you. The dying — let them die. The lost — let them go lost. And the rest — let them eat each other alive.

[10] Then I took my staff Favor and snapped it in two — breaking the covenant I had made with all the peoples.

[11] It was broken that day. And the miserable of the flock who were watching me knew this was the word of the LORD.

[12] Then I said to them: If it seems right to you, pay me my wages. If not, keep them. So they weighed out my wages — thirty pieces of silver.

[13] And the LORD said to me: Throw it to the potter — that magnificent price they put on me. So I took the thirty pieces of silver and threw them into the house of the LORD, to the potter.

[14] Then I snapped my second staff, Union, in two — severing the brotherhood between Judah and Israel.

[15] Then the LORD said to me: Now take up the gear of a worthless shepherd.

[16] Because I am raising up a shepherd in this land who will not care for the perishing, will not search for the scattered, will not heal the wounded, will not feed the healthy — but will devour the fat ones and tear off even their hooves.

[17] Woe to the worthless shepherd who abandons the flock! May the sword fall on his arm and on his right eye. May his arm wither to nothing. May his right eye go utterly dark.

[1] A dispatch. The word of the LORD concerning Israel. This is the One who stretched out the sky, who laid the earth's foundation, who shaped the human spirit inside a person — and this is what he says:

[2] Watch — I am turning Jerusalem into a cup that makes every surrounding nation reel like drunks. When the siege closes on Jerusalem, it will close on Judah too.

[3] On that day I will make Jerusalem a boulder no nation can lift. Everyone who tries will tear themselves open trying. And every nation on earth will be there, gathered against her.

[4] On that day — this is the LORD speaking — I will strike every warhorse with panic and every rider with madness. I will keep my eyes open over the house of Judah, but every enemy horse I will strike blind.

[5] Then Judah's clan leaders will say in their hearts: The people of Jerusalem are our strength — because the LORD of Armies is their God.

[6] On that day I will make Judah's chiefs like a firepan blazing among logs, like a torch dropped in dry straw. They will burn through the nations on every side, left and right. And Jerusalem will remain — right where she has always stood — Jerusalem.

[7] The LORD will rescue Judah's tents first, so the glory of David's house and the people of Jerusalem cannot tower over the rest of Judah.

[8] On that day the LORD will shield the people of Jerusalem. The weakest stumbler among them will fight like David, and David's house will be like God himself — like the angel of the LORD leading them.

[9] On that day I will set out to destroy every nation that comes against Jerusalem.
